target.title: fix(daemon): escape systemd cgroup for update handoff via systemd-run --scope
target.body:
## Summary

On systemd-supervised installs, clicking "Update now" silently fails: the update handoff helper is created but immediately killed by systemd's `KillMode=control-group` when the gateway unit restarts. The helper never executes, the version stays unchanged, and the UI shows "Update skipped: managed-service-handoff-started."

This PR detects systemd supervision and launches the handoff helper via `systemd-run --user --scope` to escape the gateway's cgroup. Non-systemd supervisors (launchd, etc.) continue using the existing detached-child path.

## Root cause

systemd's `KillMode=control-group` (the default) kills all processes in the service's cgroup on restart — including detached child processes. The handoff helper, despite being `unref()`'d and spawned with `detached: true`, stays in the same cgroup and is terminated before it can run `npm install`.

## Fix

```
systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-<handoffId> --collect -- node handoff.cjs handoff.json
```

Key design choices:

**`--scope` not `--service`**: `--scope` inherits the caller's environment (profile, state dir, config, PATH, proxy, package manager discovery all preserved). `--service` runs in a clean systemd environment and would lose all service env vars.

**`KillMode=control-group` preserved**: The scoped update helper escapes via `systemd-run --scope` into its own cgroup, so `KillMode=control-group` safely stops the gateway and all its child workers without affecting the handoff. No change to existing shutdown semantics.

**Launch failure detection**: After spawning `systemd-run`, the code awaits its exit code with a 5s timeout. If systemd-run exits non-zero (user session unavailable, D-Bus down, etc.), an error is thrown before reporting "handoff started" — preventing the gateway from exiting 80 and leaving the service stale.

**No secret exposure via argv**: The `env` object is passed via `spawn()` option — identical to the existing non-systemd path. No `--setenv=KEY=*** args are used. Secrets stay in `/proc/PID/environ` (restricted to same UID), not in `/proc/PID/cmdline` (world-readable).

Additional hardening:
- `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` strips non-`[a-zA-Z0-9:._-]` chars from `handoffId`
- `/tmp/openclaw-update-in-progress` marker file for `ExecStartPre` defense-in-depth guard
- `--collect` flag ensures scope units are garbage-collected after exit

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `update-managed-service-handoff.ts` | Detect `supervisor === "systemd"` → spawn via `systemd-run --user --scope`; await exit code to detect launch failures; add marker file lifecycle; add `sanitizeSystemdUnitName()` |
| `update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts` | 3 new tests: systemd `--scope` spawn path with env assertions, ENOENT detection, non-zero exit detection; enhanced mock with `mockChild()` helper |
| `systemd-unit.ts` | Add `ExecStartPre` marker guard, `RestartPreventExitStatus=80`; keep `KillMode=control-group` with updated comment |
| `systemd-unit.test.ts` | Updated KillMode assertion to verify `control-group` |

## Tests

```
$ npx vitest run --project gateway-methods src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts
 ✓ gateway-methods (8 tests) 632ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  8 passed (8)

$ npx vitest run --project unit-fast src/daemon/systemd-unit.test.ts
 ✓ unit-fast (4 tests) 2ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  4 passed (4)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: systemd `KillMode=control-group` kills detached update handoff helper on gateway restart, preventing self-update.
- **Real environment tested**: OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.19.13-orbstack, aarch64) with full user-level systemd and `loginctl enable-linger`. Node.js v24.15.0.

### Live systemd proof: `--scope` escapes cgroup, helper survives service restart

Created a systemd user service with `KillMode=control-group` (matching OpenClaw's generated unit at `src/daemon/systemd-unit.ts:86`):

```ini
[Service]
Type=simple
ExecStart=/tmp/handoff-test/gateway-sim.sh
KillMode=control-group
Restart=no
```

**Test A: WITH `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR)**

```
=== Gateway running ===
     Active: active (running) since Wed 2026-05-20 20:08:58 CST
   Main PID: 2797 (gateway-sim.sh)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-1000.slice/user@1000.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway-test.service

=== Helper started (in separate scope unit, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2799 started in scope at Wed May 20 08:08:58 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s while gateway lives...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:00 ===
Gateway service stopped

=== Immediately after kill ===
Helper still running (no sentinel yet — helper still working)

=== After 10s wait (helper finished at 20:09:06) ===
Helper: still alive after 8s! Gateway should be dead by now
Helper: writing sentinel at Wed May 20 08:09:06 PM CST 2026
Sentinel: update-complete
```

**Result**: Helper survived gateway service kill and completed work ✅

**Test B: WITHOUT `systemd-run --scope` (current main behavior)**

Same setup but using plain `bash &` with `disown` (simulating current detached spawn):

```
=== Helper started (no scope, sleeping 8s) ===
Helper PID=2844 (NO SCOPE) started at Wed May 20 08:09:44 PM CST 2026
Helper: sleeping 8s...

=== KILLING GATEWAY (KillMode=control-group) at 20:09:46 ===

=== After 10s wait ===
Helper log: stopped at "sleeping 8s..." — no further output
Sentinel: FAILED: helper was KILLED by KillMode=control-group!
```

**Result**: Helper was killed when gateway service stopped ❌

### Comparison

| Scenario | Helper survives service stop? | Sentinel written? |
|----------|------------------------------|-------------------|
| `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR) | ✅ Yes — ran in separate scope unit | ✅ `update-complete` |
| Plain detached child (current main) | ❌ No — killed by `KillMode=control-group` | ❌ Never created |

### Security: env via spawn(), not argv

```typescript
// env passed to spawn() for systemd-run — not in command-line args
child = spawn("systemd-run", [...], { cwd: params.root, env, detached: true, stdio: "ignore" });
```

Test coverage verifies explicitly:
```typescript
expect(options.env).toBeDefined();
expect(args).not.toContain("--setenv");
```

### What was not tested

- Full end-to-end `openclaw update` flow (requires published package version delta and npm registry)
- Live `ExecStartPre` marker file guard (defense-in-depth only, verified by code inspection)
- `--collect` garbage collection timing (non-functional, only prevents stale unit name conflicts)

Closes #84068


---

### Independent verification: real OpenClaw systemd install (2026-05-21)

Tested on a separate OrbStack Linux VM (Ubuntu 22.04 Jammy, kernel 6.17.8-orbstack, aarch64), systemd 249, Node.js v24.15.0. OpenClaw 2026.5.19 installed globally via `npm i -g openclaw`, registered as systemd user service via `openclaw gateway install`.

**Setup**: Local verdaccio registry with `openclaw@2026.5.20` as update target. Patched `startManagedServiceUpdateHandoff` to use `systemd-run --user --scope` (this PR's mechanism). Handoff helper waits for gateway death, runs `openclaw update --yes`, reinstalls service, and restarts gateway.

**Pre-conditions**:
```
$ systemctl --user status openclaw-gateway.service
● openclaw-gateway.service - OpenClaw Gateway (v2026.5.19)
     Active: active (running)
     CGroup: /user.slice/user-501.slice/user@501.service/app.slice/openclaw-gateway.service
             └─3730 /usr/bin/node .../openclaw/dist/index.js gateway --port 18789

$ grep KillMode ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
KillMode=control-group

$ grep RestartPreventExitStatus ~/.config/systemd/user/openclaw-gateway.service
RestartPreventExitStatus=78
```

**Scope launch + cgroup separation**:
```
$ systemd-run --user --scope --unit openclaw-update-proof --collect \
    -- bash /tmp/handoff-helper.sh 3730
Running scope as unit: openclaw-update-proof.scope

Gateway cgroup: .../openclaw-gateway.service
Helper cgroup:  .../openclaw-update-proof.scope   ← DIFFERENT
```

**Helper survived gateway kill**:
```
$ systemctl --user stop openclaw-gateway.service
$ kill -0 4062   # helper PID — exits 0, helper is ALIVE ✅
```

**Helper completed update + restarted gateway**:
```
[12:12:58] Handoff helper started (PID 4062)
[12:13:01] Gateway exited. Starting update...
[12:13:01] Running: openclaw update --yes --json
           → npm i -g openclaw@latest: added 361 packages in 56s (exitCode 0)
[12:13:59] Reinstalling gateway service...
[12:14:00] Restarting gateway service...
[12:14:03] Final: openclaw-gateway.service active (running), PID 4403
[12:14:03] Handoff complete!
```

| Process | Cgroup | Killed by KillMode? |
|---------|--------|---------------------|
| Gateway (PID 3730) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (intended) |
| Helper via `systemd-run --scope` (PID 4062) | `.../openclaw-update-proof.scope` | ❌ No (survives!) |
| Helper via `detached: true` (current main) | `.../openclaw-gateway.service` | ✅ Yes (bug!) |

**What was not tested in this independent run**: Full version bump (swap step hit EEXIST symlink conflict unrelated to cgroup mechanism; npm install itself succeeded). Exit code 78 restart prevention (verified by unit file content, not live).

target.title: feat(whatsapp): add dmRequireMention for DM trigger gating
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds `dmRequireMention` config option to WhatsApp channel (and base `ExtensionChannelConfig`) that gates DM replies behind mention-pattern matching
- Reuses existing `mentionPatterns` + `resolveMentionGating` infrastructure — no new pattern config needed
- When enabled, authorized DM senders can chat without triggering a reply on every message; only messages matching a mention pattern trigger the bot

Closes #61607

## Changes

| File | What |
|------|------|
| `src/config/types.channels.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `ExtensionChannelConfig` |
| `src/config/types.whatsapp.ts` | Add `dmRequireMention` to `WhatsAppSharedConfig` |
| `src/config/zod-schema.providers-whatsapp.ts` | Add Zod validation for `dmRequireMention` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/accounts.ts` | Thread `dmRequireMention` through `ResolvedWhatsAppAccount` |
| `extensions/whatsapp/src/auto-reply/monitor/on-message.ts` | DM mention gating logic in the DM processing branch |
| Generated files | Config baseline hash, channel metadata, base schema |

## Example config

```json
{
  "channels": {
    "whatsapp": {
      "dmRequireMention": true
    }
  },
  "agents": {
    "list": [
      {
        "id": "main",
        "groupChat": {
          "mentionPatterns": ["@openclaw"]
        }
      }
    ]
  }
}
```

## Test plan

- [x] TypeScript type check passes (no new errors)
- [x] `mention-gating.test.ts` passes (core gating logic)
- [x] `accounts.test.ts` passes (account config resolution)
- [x] `config-accessors.test.ts` passes
- [x] Config schema drift checks pass (`config:docs:check`, `config:channels:check`, `check:base-config-schema`)
- [ ] Manual verification: set `dmRequireMention: true`, send DM without mention pattern → no reply
- [ ] Manual verification: send DM with mention pattern → reply triggers

> **Note:** Claude and GPT were used to help scope this problem, describe the issue, and outline the implementation approach.

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: CI: report circular dependencies in PRs
target.body:
## Summary

Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets:

- Problem: We did not have a repo-standard way to detect and inspect runtime circular imports across `src`, `extensions`, and `scripts`.
- Why it matters: These cycles can drag large strongly connected components into the runtime graph and make lazy/runtime boundaries harder to preserve.
- What changed: I added `dependency-cruiser` repo tooling, a focused circular-dependency config, a local report script, and a non-blocking CI job that publishes PR summaries plus raw artifacts.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): I did not start failing CI on existing cycles and I did not refactor any of the current cycle clusters in this PR.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [x] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [x] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`.

- Root cause: N/A
- Missing detection / guardrail: N/A
- Contributing context (if known): N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`.

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [ ] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: N/A
- Scenario the test should lock in: N/A
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: This is tooling/CI wiring, so the direct guardrail is running the report script and validating the emitted artifacts/summary locally.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: The value here is report generation rather than product behavior. I verified the report output directly.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

PRs now get a non-blocking `dependency-circular-report` job that publishes:

- a summary table of cycle-heavy scopes
- a sorted descending directory/module list
- a Mermaid visualization for scope distribution and largest SCC clusters
- uploaded artifacts with raw JSON and full cycle listings

Local development also gets:

- `pnpm deps:circular`
- `pnpm deps:circular:json`
- `pnpm deps:circular:report`

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[PR] -> [standard CI lanes only] -> [no circular-import visibility]

After:
[PR] -> [dependency-circular-report job] -> [step summary + artifacts]
     -> [sorted scopes/directories + SCC graphs]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: CI now runs `dependency-cruiser` against repo sources to generate reports. This is limited to checked-in code in the Actions workspace and does not expand runtime privileges beyond normal repo CI tooling.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node.js v25.8.1 local shell
- Model/provider: N/A
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): default repo config

### Steps

1. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular`
2. Run `npm exec pnpm -- run deps:circular:report`
3. Inspect `.artifacts/dependency-cruiser/step-summary.md`, `report.md`, `summary.json`, and `cycle-violations.txt`

### Expected

- `dependency-cruiser` finds current runtime cycles using the repo config
- the report script emits a readable sorted summary plus raw artifacts
- CI can reuse the same script to publish a non-blocking PR report

### Actual

- Local runs completed and emitted the expected artifact set
- Current snapshot found `150` cycle violations, `24` cyclic SCCs, and `195` modules participating in cycles
- Top cycle-heavy scopes were `extensions/browser`, `src/agents`, `extensions/mattermost`, `extensions/zalouser`, and `src/cli`

## Evidence

Attach at least one:

- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

- Verified scenarios: I ran the circular check locally, ran the report generator locally, and inspected the generated markdown/JSON/text artifacts for sorting, counts, and representative SCC output.
- Edge cases checked: I confirmed type-only imports are excluded, test files are excluded, the report groups by scope and exact directory, and the summary stays non-blocking.
- What you did **not** verify: I did not yet verify the rendered GitHub Actions step summary or artifact download UX inside a live PR run before opening this PR.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) No
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps:

## Risks and Mitigations

List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`.

- Risk: The report could be noisy or too broad for reviewers if the summary is not distilled enough.
  - Mitigation: The PR summary shows only top scopes/directories/SCCs, while the full raw listing stays in artifacts.
- Risk: The report job adds CI time.
  - Mitigation: It is non-blocking, uses a focused config, and runs in the same lightweight Node environment as other CI checks.

target.title: Policy: add agent workspace conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a policy-only conformance surface for locking agent workspace posture down to read-only/no-write configurations.

- records agent/default sandbox `workspaceAccess` as policy evidence
- adds `agents.workspace.allowedAccess` and `agents.workspace.denyTools` checks
- documents the supported read-only policy shape and evidence payload

This is stacked after #81981 and intentionally stays at config/policy conformance level; it does not add runtime enforcement.

## Verification

- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm docs:list`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules/@typescript/native-preview/bin/tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-agent-workspace.tsbuildinfo`
- `/mnt/c/src/claws-hapi/.agents/skills/autoreview/scripts/autoreview --mode local --reviewer codex --fallback-reviewer none`

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Policy conformance can now require agent sandbox workspace access to stay within an allowlist and require explicit deny coverage for shell/process/file-mutation tools.
Real environment tested: WSL checkout at `/root/src/openclaw-policy-agent-workspace` on Ubuntu-24.04.
Exact steps or command run after this patch: Ran focused policy doctor/CLI tests, extension test typecheck, docs listing, diff whitespace check, and Codex autoreview from the WSL checkout.
Evidence after fix: 115 focused policy tests passed; extension test typecheck completed successfully; docs list completed successfully; autoreview completed with no accepted/actionable findings after fixing its one accepted finding.
Observed result after fix: Malformed agent workspace policy reports policy shape errors, nonconforming workspace/tool posture reports policy findings, and read-only policy with group deny coverage is accepted.
What was not tested: No runtime sandbox/tool enforcement was tested because this PR only adds config-level policy conformance.

target.title: Policy: add gateway exposure checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Policy 1.0 Gateway exposure conformance category with config-only evidence and doctor/lint findings.

Logical base: #81974 after skipping #81104. This PR intentionally does not include runtime audit, gateway protocol, approval metadata, Swift protocol, or before-tool-call changes.

- Records Gateway bind/auth/rate-limit/Control UI/Tailscale/remote/HTTP posture as policy evidence.
- Enforces non-loopback bind, disabled auth, missing explicit rate-limit posture, insecure Control UI toggles, Tailscale Funnel, remote mode, HTTP endpoints, and unrestricted URL-fetch posture.
- Documents the new Gateway policy rules and evidence shape.

## Safety

This is config-level conformance only. `doctor --lint` and `policy check` read existing OpenClaw config and report findings. This PR does not launch a Gateway, mutate Gateway config, change Gateway runtime behavior, or add runtime enforcement.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: adds policy conformance evidence and findings for Gateway, Control UI, WebChat, and HTTP exposure settings, including unsafe remote binding, disabled auth, missing rate-limit posture, insecure Control UI toggles, Tailscale Funnel, remote gateway mode, enabled HTTP endpoints, and unrestricted URL-fetch posture.

Real environment tested: Windows source worktree restacked on signed #81974 head `3607c62364`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node --no-maglev node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules\@typescript\native-preview\bin\tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-81981-config.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx -- docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `git diff --check --cached`

Evidence after fix:

![PR #81981 Gateway exposure policy before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-81981-fresh/pr-81981/pr-81981-gateway-exposure-policy-before-after-proof.png)

Observed result after fix: focused policy doctor/CLI tests passed 2 files and 80 tests. Extension tsgo, docs MDX, and diff whitespace checks passed. Head commit `3fffcd1cbe` is GitHub-verified signed.

What was not tested: no live Gateway server was launched for this proof image. The proof verifies policy doctor/lint behavior against config/policy files.

## Related

- #80783 — Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
- #81974 — Policy: add secrets auth provenance checks

target.title: fix(update): harden managed handoff cwd
target.body:
## Summary
- run managed service update helper and updater processes from an existing stable cwd instead of a package root/current cwd that may have disappeared during self-update
- make the early `isMainModule(...)` startup guard tolerate `ENOENT`/`uv_cwd` when the process cwd has disappeared
- add regression coverage for both the handoff cwd and early startup cwd fallback paths

## Verification
- node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts src/infra/is-main.ts
- node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts
- node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr83875-real-smoke-worktree.mjs
- git diff --check

## Not Run
- Live macOS LaunchAgent managed-update smoke; this environment is Linux and cannot exercise launchd.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Managed package update handoff should keep working when the gateway process cwd or package root disappears, and early startup `isMainModule(...)` should not throw when `process.cwd()` raises `ENOENT`/`uv_cwd`.
- Real environment tested: Linux 6.19.14+kali-amd64 isolated worktree at pushed commit `d7314b2295`; Node v22.22.2; pnpm 11.1.0. The smoke removed the live process cwd to trigger `uv_cwd`, then started a real managed update handoff helper with a missing package root and a harmless mock update script.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts src/infra/is-main.ts`
  2. `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts`
  3. `node --import tsx /tmp/openclaw-pr83875-real-smoke-worktree.mjs`
- Evidence after fix: Terminal stdout from the real smoke command:
  ```json
  {
    "cwdThrows": true,
    "isMainResult": true,
    "missingPackageRootExists": false,
    "handoffStatus": "started",
    "handoffPidType": "number",
    "handoffCommand": "openclaw update --yes --timeout 1",
    "logContainsExitZero": true,
    "commandCwd": "/home/rev",
    "commandCwdExists": true,
    "commandCwdIsMissingPackageRoot": false,
    "logExcerpt": "[2026-05-19T04:07:11.241Z] starting managed update command: openclaw update --yes --timeout 1
managed-command-cwd=/home/rev
managed-command-argv=["update","--yes","--json","--timeout","1"]
[2026-05-19T04:07:11.268Z] managed update command exited code=0 signal=null"
  }
  ```
  Targeted checks also passed: oxlint reported `Found 0 warnings and 0 errors`; vitest reported `Test Files 5 passed (5)` and `Tests 55 passed (55)`.
- Observed result after fix: With the current process cwd removed, `process.cwd()` threw `ENOENT` but `isMainModule(...)` returned true using the current-file directory fallback. The managed handoff started from a missing package root, selected an existing stable cwd (`/home/rev`) instead of the missing root, ran the mock update command there, and the helper log recorded `managed update command exited code=0 signal=null`.
- What was not tested: Did not run a live macOS LaunchAgent, Linux systemd user service, or Windows scheduled task update. The smoke used the same handoff helper path with a harmless mock update command instead of running a real package update.

Fixes openclaw/openclaw#83808

## Current-head proof refresh (2026-05-19)

Pushed commit `0fa523cee2c99aa661ca21472bacaf2529fd138b` closes the remaining cwd-path blocker: `runGatewayUpdate` now tolerates `process.cwd()` throwing `ENOENT: uv_cwd` in both update-runner candidate gathering and npm install environment setup, then proceeds from the supplied update cwd.

Commands run at that head:
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs src/infra/npm-install-env.ts src/infra/update-runner.ts src/infra/update-runner.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts src/infra/is-main.ts` -> 0 warnings, 0 errors.
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/infra/npm-install-env.ts src/infra/update-runner.ts src/infra/update-runner.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts src/infra/is-main.ts` -> all matched files use the correct format.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/infra/update-runner.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update-managed-service-handoff.test.ts src/gateway/server-methods/update.test.ts src/infra/is-main.test.ts` -> 6 test files passed, 99 tests passed.
- `git diff --check` -> passed.

Regression proof detail: the new `runGatewayUpdate` test mocks `process.cwd()` to throw `ENOENT: uv_cwd` and verifies the real update-runner path still probes the supplied `cwd` (`git -C <tempDir> rev-parse --show-toplevel`) and exits cleanly as `skipped/no-upstream` rather than crashing before handoff.

Not run: live macOS LaunchAgent managed-update smoke; this Linux host cannot exercise launchd.

Independent diff review passed with no security concerns or logic errors.

target.title: fix(ui): preserve WebChat messages across session switches
target.body:
## Summary
- Fixes #80855.
- Cache visible WebChat messages per session when switching conversations, then restore cached messages immediately when switching back before chat.history returns.
- Cache final assistant messages for switched-away sessions so subagent/report events are not dropped while another conversation is selected.

## Real behavior proof
- Behavior addressed: WebChat session switching should not leave the restored conversation blank, and final assistant/subagent messages for a switched-away session should not be silently dropped.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw checkout on Linux with production TypeScript UI modules executed through Node 22.22.2 and tsx; no test runner or mocks were used for the proof commands.
- Exact steps or command run after the patch: Ran two node --import tsx commands from the repository root. One imported switchChatSession from ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.ts and switched main -> other -> main with real session state. The other imported handleChatEvent from ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts and delivered a final event for agent:main:webchat while main was selected.
- Evidence after fix:

```text
$ node --import tsx -e '<imports switchChatSession from ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.ts and switches main -> other -> main>'
{
  "activeSession": "main",
  "visibleMessagesAfterSwitchBack": [
    "main report before switch"
  ],
  "cachedOtherMessages": [
    "other reply before switch back"
  ]
}
```

```text
$ node --import tsx -e '<imports handleChatEvent from ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.ts and sends final event for agent:main:webchat while main is selected>'
{
  "result": null,
  "currentSession": "main",
  "currentMessages": [
    "main visible"
  ],
  "cachedWebchatMessages": [
    "subagent report while switched away"
  ]
}
```

- Observed result after fix: Switching back to main immediately restored "main report before switch" instead of an empty message list, and a final message for the switched-away WebChat session was retained in cachedWebchatMessages while the currently visible main session stayed unchanged.
- What was not tested: No browser screenshot or live multi-channel WSL2 deployment was captured; the proof exercises the production UI state-transition modules directly.

## Verification
- RED: corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts failed on the new switch-back preservation test before implementation.
- RED: corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts failed on the new switched-away final-event cache test before implementation.
- GREEN: corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts
- GREEN: corepack pnpm test ui/src/ui/app-render.helpers.node.test.ts ui/src/ui/controllers/chat.test.ts
- GREEN: PATH=/tmp/openclaw-corepack-shims:$PATH pnpm check:changed -- --base origin/main
- GREEN: git diff --check

target.title: Policy: add secret and auth conformance checks
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a Policy 1.0 secrets/auth provenance category with redacted config-derived evidence and doctor/lint findings.

Logical base: #80783 after the signed oc-path evidence fix. This PR intentionally skips #81104 and does not include runtime audit, gateway protocol, approval metadata, Swift protocol, or before-tool-call changes.

- Records secret provider/input provenance without exposing secret values.
- Enforces `secrets.disallowInline`, `secrets.requireManagedProviders`, `secrets.denySources`, and `secrets.allowInsecureProviders`.
- Adds auth profile metadata/mode checks with `auth.profiles.requireMetadata` and `auth.profiles.allowModes`.
- Documents the new policy rules and JSON evidence shape.

## Safety

This is config-level conformance only. `doctor --lint` and `policy check` read existing OpenClaw config and report findings. This PR does not mutate config, contact secret providers, read secret values, change credential resolution, or add runtime enforcement.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: adds policy conformance evidence and findings for configured secret/auth provenance without exposing raw secret material, including inline secrets, unmanaged SecretRef providers, denied provider sources, insecure secret providers, missing auth-profile metadata, and unapproved auth-profile modes.

Real environment tested: Windows source worktree restacked on the signed #80783 head `66495f031f`.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

- `node --no-maglev node_modules\vitest\vitest.mjs extensions/policy/src/doctor/register.test.ts extensions/policy/src/cli.test.ts --run --reporter=dot`
- `node node_modules\@typescript\native-preview\bin\tsgo.js -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.extensions.test.json --incremental --tsBuildInfoFile .artifacts/tsgo-cache/extensions-test-policy-81974-config.tsbuildinfo`
- `pnpm docs:check-mdx -- docs/cli/policy.md docs/plugins/reference/policy.md`
- `git diff --check giodl73-repo/policy-conformance-examples...HEAD`

Evidence after fix:

![PR #81974 secrets/auth provenance before/after proof](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/giodl73-repo/openclaw/proof-artifacts/pr-81974-fresh/pr-81974/pr-81974-secrets-auth-provenance-before-after-proof.png)

Observed result after fix: focused policy doctor/CLI tests passed 2 files and 79 tests. Extension tsgo, docs MDX, and diff whitespace checks passed. Head commit `3607c62364` is GitHub-verified signed.

What was not tested: no live external vault or credential provider was contacted. The proof verifies policy doctor/lint behavior against config/policy files and redaction behavior.

## Related

- #80783 — Policy: add model, network, and MCP conformance checks
- #81981 — Policy: add gateway exposure checks

target.title: fix(security): escape entry.id in HTML export to prevent attribute XSS
target.body:
## Problem

`template.js` in the HTML export feature interpolates `entry.id` directly into HTML attribute strings without escaping:

```javascript
// Line 1312 (copy-link button)
`<button class="copy-link-btn" data-entry-id="${entryId}" ...>`

// Line 1323 (entry wrapper)
const entryId = `entry-${entry.id}`;
```

If an `entry.id` contains double-quote characters (e.g., a crafted conversation export or corrupted data), the quote closes the `data-entry-id` attribute and the remainder is parsed as new HTML attributes. An attacker-controlled `entry.id` of `x" onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)` injects an `onmouseover` event handler into the DOM.

This is a stored XSS in the HTML export: the payload persists in the exported HTML file and fires whenever a user interacts with the affected element.

## Fix

Wrap both `entry.id` interpolation sites with the existing `escapeHtmlAttr()` function (already defined in `template.js` for other attributes):

```javascript
// Line 1312: escapeHtmlAttr(entryId)
`<button class="copy-link-btn" data-entry-id="${escapeHtmlAttr(entryId)}" ...>`

// Line 1323: escapeHtmlAttr(entry.id)
const entryId = `entry-${escapeHtmlAttr(entry.id)}`;
```

`escapeHtmlAttr` replaces `"`, `&`, `<`, `>`, and `'` with their HTML entity equivalents (`"`, `&`, `<`, `>`, `'`). This prevents quote characters from closing the attribute and injecting new HTML attributes.

Production diff is 2 lines changed in `template.js`, plus 389 lines of security test coverage.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: `entry.id` was interpolated raw into HTML attributes in the export template. A crafted entry ID with double quotes broke out of the `data-entry-id` attribute and injected arbitrary HTML attributes (XSS).

Real environment tested: Linux (Ubuntu 24.04, WSL2), Node.js v22.16.0, OpenClaw built from source at `/tmp/fix-83104`. DOM rendering via linkedom (the same DOM engine used by OpenClaw's own test suite) running the production `template.js` in a V8 VM context.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:
```bash
# 1. Build OpenClaw from patched source
cd /tmp/fix-83104
CI=true pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
pnpm build

# 2. Generate an HTML export with a malicious entry.id
# Session data with entry.id = 'x" onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)'
# injected as base64 into the template, producing a 247KB HTML file
node -e '<proof script: reads template files, creates session with XSS payload,
         replaces placeholders, writes /tmp/xss-proof-export.html>'

# 3. Parse the generated HTML with linkedom + V8 VM and inspect the DOM
# Runs the production template.js (with escapeHtmlAttr) in a VM context,
# then queries the rendered DOM for onmouseover attributes
node --input-type=module -e '<DOM inspection script>'

# 4. Repeat step 3 with escapeHtmlAttr calls reverted to raw entry.id
# to show the vulnerability without the fix
node --input-type=module -e '<same script but templateJs.replace(escapeHtmlAttr(...), entry.id)>'

# 5. Run the security test suite
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts

# 6. Start the patched gateway
timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
```

Evidence after fix: terminal output copied below.

### Red-green DOM proof: XSS payload in entry.id

The proof generates a full 247KB HTML export with a malicious `entry.id` of `x" onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)"`, then parses it with linkedom and queries the DOM for XSS breakout.

**AFTER fix (with `escapeHtmlAttr`):**

```console
=== AFTER FIX (with escapeHtmlAttr) ===
Injected entry.id: "x\" onmouseover=\"alert(document.cookie)"

Elements with onmouseover handler: 0
XSS breakout: NO -- SAFE

Copy-link buttons: 2
  data-entry-id value: "x\" onmouseover=\"alert(document.cookie)"
  data-entry-id value: "safe-2"

Raw HTML around copy-link-btn:
ument.cookie)"><button class="copy-link-btn" data-entry-id="x" onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)" title="Copy link to this message">
```

The `"` entities in `data-entry-id="x" onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)"` keep the entire payload inside the attribute value. No `onmouseover` attribute exists in the DOM. The querySelectorAll check confirms 0 elements with event handlers.

**BEFORE fix (escapeHtmlAttr reverted to raw `entry.id`):**

```console
=== BEFORE FIX (escapeHtmlAttr reverted to raw entry.id) ===
Injected entry.id: "x\" onmouseover=\"alert(document.cookie)"

Elements with onmouseover handler: 1
XSS breakout: YES -- VULNERABLE

Copy-link buttons: 2
  data-entry-id: "x\" onmouseover=\"alert(document.cookie)"
  data-entry-id: "safe-2"
```

Without `escapeHtmlAttr`, the raw double-quote in `entry.id` closes the `data-entry-id` attribute. The `onmouseover="alert(document.cookie)"` becomes a real DOM attribute. `querySelectorAll("[onmouseover]")` finds 1 element. In a real browser, hovering that element would execute `alert(document.cookie)`.

### How the DOM proof works

1. **Template rendering**: The proof reads the production `template.html`, `template.css`, `template.js`, and vendor scripts from the source tree. It creates session data with a malicious `entry.id`, base64-encodes it, and injects it into the template placeholders (the same injection the gateway performs when generating exports).

2. **VM execution**: The rendered HTML is parsed with `linkedom` (OpenClaw's own DOM library). The production `template.js` is executed in a V8 VM context with the parsed document. This runs the exact same code path as a real browser opening the export.

3. **DOM inspection**: After the template JS renders the conversation, `querySelectorAll("[onmouseover]")` checks whether the XSS payload broke out of the attribute into a real event handler. This is a definitive check: if the count is 0, the payload is contained; if >0, XSS is confirmed.

4. **Red-green comparison**: The "before" proof string-replaces `escapeHtmlAttr(entry.id)` back to `entry.id` in the template JS, re-runs the same rendering, and shows the onmouseover handler appears in the DOM.

### Live gateway startup proof

```console
$ timeout 10 node openclaw.mjs gateway
2026-05-21T14:08:04.340-07:00 [gateway] loading configuration…
2026-05-21T14:08:04.397-07:00 [gateway] starting...
2026-05-21T14:08:06.725-07:00 [health-monitor] started (interval: 300s, startup-grace: 60s, channel-connect-grace: 120s)
2026-05-21T14:08:07.655-07:00 [gateway] agent model: openai/gpt-5.5 (thinking=medium, fast=off)
2026-05-21T14:08:07.656-07:00 [gateway] http server listening (8 plugins; 3.3s)
2026-05-21T14:08:08.174-07:00 [gateway] ready
2026-05-21T14:08:13.914-07:00 [gateway] received SIGTERM; shutting down
2026-05-21T14:08:14.016-07:00 [shutdown] completed cleanly in 94ms
```

### Compiled code verification

`escapeHtmlAttr` is present in the compiled production bundle:

```console
$ grep -c "escapeHtmlAttr" dist/format-DbIGvGi1.js
2
```

Both call sites (copy-link button `data-entry-id` and entry wrapper ID) are protected in the compiled output.

### Vitest security test suite (10 tests, supplemental)

```console
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/export-html/template.security.test.ts
 ✓ |auto-reply| template.security.test.ts (10 tests) 169ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Observed result after fix: The `escapeHtmlAttr()` function converts `"` to `"` in both `entry.id` interpolation sites. The DOM proof confirms: with the fix, 0 elements have `onmouseover` handlers (XSS contained). Without the fix, 1 element has an `onmouseover` handler (XSS breakout). The gateway builds and starts cleanly with the patched export template.

What was not tested: Opening the exported HTML in a real browser (Chromium, Firefox). The linkedom DOM proof exercises the same rendering code path and uses the definitive `querySelectorAll("[onmouseover]")` check. A real browser would additionally show the `alert()` dialog on hover, but the DOM attribute presence is the authoritative XSS indicator.

target.title: fix(slack): deliver native plugin approvals
target.body:
## Summary

- deliver Slack native plugin approval events through the existing native approval runtime and Block Kit approval UI
- route Slack plugin approval DMs to plugin approvers from `allowFrom` / account defaults while keeping exec approval DMs on exec approvers
- align Slack plugin fallback suppression with kind-aware native deliverability, and document the Slack native approval behavior

## Verification

- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/approval-native.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/events/interactions.test.ts extensions/slack/src/monitor/provider.allowlist.test.ts extensions/slack/src/send.blocks.test.ts` passed: 5 files, 100 tests.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts` passed: 1 file, 7 tests, including the plugin approval actions `Allow Once`, `Allow Always`, and `Deny`.
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/qa-lab/src/codex-plugin-lifecycle.test.ts extensions/slack/src/approval-handler.runtime.test.ts` passed: 2 files, 16 tests.
- `git diff --check` passed.
- `uvx showboat verify .mem/main/proofs/demo-12-dev-slack-native-plugin-approval/raw/showboat-summary.md` passed.
- `node scripts/check-deadcode-unused-files.mjs` no longer reports the qa-lab Codex fixture files after renaming them to `*.fixture.ts`; the broader local run is still blocked by unrelated existing `extensions/openai-apps` unused-file findings.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Slack native approval delivery now handles plugin approval request, resolved, and expired events instead of suppressing shared plugin fallback while subscribing only to exec events. The Slack send receipt also records the channel returned by Slack for DM/user-target sends so resolved approval updates can `chat.update` the delivered message.

Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw source checkout on branch `codex/slack-native-plugin-approval-events`, dev Slack profile, Slack socket mode connected to the configured workspace, `channels.slack.execApprovals.enabled: false`, and global `approvals.plugin` enabled with Slack as a plugin approval target.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Started the dev gateway with the dev profile, asked the dev Codex agent `open 1 password with computer use`, captured plugin approval `plugin:b127fe77-b624-473e-b1ef-70a054098bdc`, resolved it through the gateway `plugin.approval.resolve` path, and verified the pending/resolved Slack messages plus gateway wait decision with `uvx showboat verify .mem/main/proofs/demo-12-dev-slack-native-plugin-approval/raw/showboat-summary.md`.

Evidence after fix: Showboat proof `.mem/main/proofs/demo-12-dev-slack-native-plugin-approval/raw/showboat-summary.md` passes and records: `PASS pending Slack message used action block`, `PASS pending Slack action labels`, `PASS pending Slack message omitted slash command fallback`, `PASS approval resolve succeeded`, `PASS resolved Slack message omitted slash command fallback`, `PASS resolved Slack message removed actions`, `PASS gateway waitDecision completed`, and `PASS latest gateway update had no message_not_found`. The local proof screenshot is `.mem/main/proofs/demo-12-dev-slack-native-plugin-approval/raw/slack-native-approval-pending-buttons-proof.png` and shows the native Slack buttons `Allow Once`, `Allow Always`, and `Deny` on the pending plugin approval card.

Observed result after fix: The plugin approval request delivered to Slack as native Block Kit UI while exec approvals were disabled, the old `Reply with: /approve <id>` fallback text was absent, the action labels were `Allow Once`, `Allow Always`, and `Deny`, resolving the approval completed `plugin.approval.waitDecision`, and Slack updated the existing approval message without `message_not_found`.

What was not tested: A full successful post-approval Computer Use action against 1Password was not completed because the downstream Codex/Computer Use run failed after approval with `auth refresh request timed out after 10s`; the approval delivery, Slack UI, action resolution, and resolved-message update path were completed and verified.

target.title: Redact exec tool result payloads
target.body:
## Summary
- redact exec foreground tool-result text and structured `details.aggregated` before returning results to the agent
- redact node-host exec stdout/stderr/error output before formatting tool results
- redact process poll/log/list output and structured details that can include output tails or aggregated process output

## Real behavior proof
**Behavior or issue addressed:** Exec and background process tool results could return secret-shaped stdout/stderr/session text back to the agent. This patch redacts those returned tool-result surfaces before they leave the exec/process/node-host formatters.

**Real environment tested:** Real local OpenClaw checkout on branch `fix/exec-tool-result-redaction` at `da701a0bbf6c`, using an isolated temporary `OPENCLAW_HOME`. The proof used an obvious fake canary value, not a real credential.

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a local runtime proof that exercised `createExecTool` and `createProcessTool` with a fake `REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET` value, then inspected the returned tool-result `content[0].text` and structured `details.aggregated` values for foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list tail/details surfaces.

**Evidence after fix:** Copied live output from the local runtime proof:

```json
{
  "foregroundExec": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processPoll": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n\n\nProcess exited with code 0.",
    "detailsAggregated": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890\n",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processLog": {
    "status": "completed",
    "text": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "detailsAggregated": "",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  },
  "processList": {
    "status": "completed",
    "textTail": "REDACTION_CANARY_SECRET=clawsw…7890",
    "rawCanaryValuePresent": false,
    "redactedMarkerPresent": true
  }
}
```

Supplemental focused regression coverage also exercises node-host exec stdout redaction by passing a fake node `system.run` payload through `executeNodeHostCommand` and asserting both returned text and `details.aggregated` redact the raw canary.

**Observed result after fix:** The raw fake canary value was absent from returned foreground exec, process poll, process log, and process list output. Redacted markers were present in the returned text/details where output was expected. The node-host formatter path is covered by the focused regression test for the same returned tool-result boundary.

**What was not tested:** Live node-host exec against a physical connected node was unavailable in this isolated proof environment; the node-host formatter was validated with the focused regression test that feeds a fake gateway node response through the production formatter path.

## Tests
- `corepack pnpm exec oxfmt --write --threads=1 src/agents/bash-tools.exec.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node-phases.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped output'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node stdout'`

Notes: a full local run of the three targeted files is currently blocked in my environment by an unrelated invalid local `~/.openclaw/openclaw.json` model alias (`openai-codex/gpt-5.3-codex`) during the pre-existing foreground timeout test. The new redaction regression tests pass individually under the agents-core Vitest config.

Fixes #71211



## Follow-up after review
- Added commit `bc79136bb7` to redact background exec `details.tail` before returning running-session tool results.
- Added regression coverage for background exec `details.tail`, process list command/tail details, and node-host stderr/error redaction in addition to stdout.

Additional local verification after follow-up:
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core`
- `corepack pnpm tsgo:core:test`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`
- `corepack pnpm exec vitest run --config test/vitest/vitest.agents-core.config.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`
- `git diff --check`

Note: the full local three-file Vitest run still hits the pre-existing environment-sensitive approval path in `exec foreground failures > returns a failed text result when the default timeout is exceeded`, where local host exec approval intercepts `sleep 5` before the timeout assertion.

## Review feedback update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by removing the JSON embedded-fallback behavior change from this PR; `openclaw agent --json` fallback behavior is back to the existing contract.
- Addressed ClawSweeper P2 by replacing the live-update process timing test with deterministic `runExecProcess` regression coverage, so the previous single-line skip branches are gone.
- Final head verified: `8793847f206cca93c49abe33e4f49b9b55a0c4dc`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts -t 'JSON'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.ts src/commands/agent-via-gateway.test.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.ts src/cli/program/register.agent.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`\n\nChanged-file gate note: `node scripts/crabbox-wrapper.mjs run --shell -- "pnpm check:changed"` did not reach pnpm because local `crabbox` failed its binary sanity check. Direct `pnpm check:changed` also could not run in this local checkout because `origin/main...HEAD` has no merge base on the grafted/shallow branch. The focused tests, lint, and diff checks above passed on the final head.

## Process control redaction update - 2026-05-21
- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 by redacting command-derived process result names for running-session controls (`write`, `submit`, `paste`) plus `kill`, `remove`, `poll`, `log`, `list`, and `send-keys`.
- Added fake-secret regression coverage for `process write` and `process send-keys` command-derived `details.name` values.
- Final head verified locally: `24ac21f4280e9e403722c271d12fda0a4c68535d4`.

Additional verification on final head:
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-foreground-failures.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-runtime.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped stdout in update text and details tail'`\n- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/bash-tools.exec-host-node.test.ts -t 'redacts secret-shaped node'`\n- `pnpm exec oxlint src/agents/bash-tools.process.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process.poll-timeout.test.ts src/agents/bash-tools.process-send-keys.test.ts`\n- `git diff --check && git diff --cached --check`

target.title: fix(google-vertex): support production ADC modes
target.body:
## Summary

- Allow native `api: "google-vertex"` configs through model API validation.
- Preserve inherited provider-level `api: "google-vertex"` during inline model materialization.
- Extend the existing Google Vertex ADC helper beyond `authorized_user` files by falling back to `google-auth-library` for production ADC modes, including GKE Workload Identity / metadata-server ADC, `external_account`, `service_account`, Cloud Run, GAE, and Compute Engine.

## Why

OpenClaw already has a native Google Vertex transport: `model.api === "google-vertex"` selects the Vertex transport path. The current ADC preflight and token resolver only accept `authorized_user` credentials files. That preserves the local `gcloud auth application-default login` case, but rejects common production GCP auth shapes before the native Vertex transport can succeed.

Production GCP deployments commonly rely on metadata server ADC, GKE Workload Identity, Workload Identity Federation (`external_account`), or service account credentials rather than a static Gemini API key or a user ADC file. This patch keeps the existing `authorized_user` refresh behavior intact and adds the standard `google-auth-library` ADC chain for the other supported runtime modes.

## Related Upstream Work

This PR is scoped as a production ADC completion for the existing native Google Vertex path, not as a new Vertex provider.

Related work:

- #9729 requested Google Vertex ADC support and was closed as implemented.
- #60860 is a broader Google Vertex provider PR that also touches ADC and endpoint routing.
- #74914 adds `api: "google-vertex"` schema/config support, but is still open. This PR includes the schema allowance and also preserves provider-level inherited `api: "google-vertex"` in `normalizeResolvedTransportApi`, since adding to `MODEL_APIS` alone only covers explicit model-level config.
- #65023 tracks a broader `google-genai` SDK provider direction.

The difference in this PR is that it keeps the existing native Vertex transport path, preserves the current `authorized_user` behavior, and extends ADC resolution to production GCP modes such as GKE Workload Identity / metadata server ADC, `external_account`, `service_account`, Cloud Run, GAE, and Compute Engine. It also includes redacted live behavior proof from a GKE-hosted OpenClaw deployment.

## Evidence

Redacted live proof from a GKE-hosted OpenClaw deployment validated the metadata-server ADC path end to end.

Environment and config facts:

```text
GOOGLE_API_KEY_present = no
GOOGLE_APPLICATION_CREDENTIALS_present = no
GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT_present = yes
GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION = us-central1
```

Rendered model config:

```text
models.providers.google.apiKey = "gcp-vertex-credentials"
models.providers.google.models[0].api = "google-vertex"
models.providers.google.models[0].id = "gemini-2.5-pro"
models.providers.google.models[0].baseUrl = "https://us-central1-aiplatform.googleapis.com"
agents.defaults.model = "google/gemini-2.5-pro"
```

Metadata-server access token evidence:

```text
[gke-metadata-server]
Fetching access token for the bound Google service account.
"/computeMetadata/v1/instance/service-accounts/default/token?scopes=https%3A%2F%2Fwww.googleapis.com%2Fauth%2Fcloud-platform" HTTP/200
```

Gateway transport evidence:

```text
[provider-transport-fetch]
[model-fetch] start provider=google api=google-vertex model=gemini-2.5-pro
method=POST
url=https://us-central1-aiplatform.googleapis.com/v1/projects/<redacted-project>/locations/us-central1/publishers/google/models/gemini-2.5-pro:streamGenerateContent
timeoutMs=120000 proxy=none policy=custom

[provider-transport-fetch]
[model-fetch] response provider=google api=google-vertex model=gemini-2.5-pro
status=200 elapsedMs=2291 contentType=text/event-stream
```

User-visible outcome:

```text
A user-visible OpenClaw response was returned through the configured channel after the Vertex `streamGenerateContent` request completed with HTTP 200.
```

This proves the patch path did not use `x-goog-api-key` or a static service-account JSON file. The deployment used the `gcp-vertex-credentials` marker and obtained a bearer token from metadata-server ADC for the native Google Vertex transport.

## Test plan

- `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile`
  - Result: passed.
- `node scripts/test-projects.mjs extensions/google/transport-stream.test.ts src/config/zod-schema.models.test.ts src/agents/pi-embedded-runner/model.inline-provider.test.ts`
  - Result: passed 3 Vitest shards after rebasing onto current `origin/main`.
  - `test/vitest/vitest.unit-fast.config.ts`: 1 test file passed, 12 tests passed.
  - `test/vitest/vitest.runtime-config.config.ts`: 1 test file passed, 1 test passed.
  - `test/vitest/vitest.extension-providers.config.ts`: 1 test file passed, 53 tests passed.
- `git diff --check`
  - Result: passed.

Made with [Cursor](https://cursor.com)

target.title: fix(gateway): normalize explicit state dir overrides at startup
target.body:
## Summary
- normalize explicit `OPENCLAW_STATE_DIR` / `CLAWDBOT_STATE_DIR` overrides to absolute paths at startup
- call the normalization from both the gateway CLI path and direct `startGatewayServer()` startup
- add focused tests for path pinning and startup ordering

## Why
This is a follow-up hardening change for #52250.

Relative state-dir overrides remain cwd-sensitive. Normalizing them at startup removes the trigger path before later workspace or cwd changes can retarget plugin/state discovery.

## Verification
- `pnpm exec vitest run --config vitest.unit.config.ts src/config/paths.test.ts src/cli/gateway-cli/run.option-collisions.test.ts`
- validated against the same standalone repro fixture at https://github.com/PerfectPan/openclaw-repro-plugin-registry-subagent by normalizing the relative state-dir override before the later cwd change
  - before: absolute state dir, channel known
  - after: absolute state dir, channel still known

target.title: chore: harden npm shrinkwrap release path
target.body:
## Summary
- Ship the root OpenClaw npm package and every OpenClaw-owned publishable npm plugin with generated `npm-shrinkwrap.json` files.
- Generate shrinkwrap from the published npm dependency graph, strip dev deps, and apply `pnpm-workspace.yaml` override policy.
- Add shrinkwrap and `package-lock.json` to the dependency security review surface through CODEOWNERS, dependency-change awareness, labeler sizing, and dependency report output.
- Enforce root shrinkwrap in release/package validators, check plugin-local shrinkwrap before plugin pack/publish, and document the root/plugin contract.

## Explainer
`npm-shrinkwrap.json` is npm's publishable lockfile. Unlike `package-lock.json`, npm can include it in published packages, so installing `openclaw` or an OpenClaw-owned npm plugin can resolve through the reviewed dependency graph that release tooling generated.

This does not replace `pnpm-lock.yaml` for source development. The repo still uses pnpm as the source-of-truth package manager. The generator derives npm shrinkwrap from each package's stock published dependency graph: root `openclaw` for the CLI/Gateway package, and `extensions/<id>/package.json` for each `publishToNpm` plugin package.

Plugin installs already run through npm's managed root, so third-party packages that ship shrinkwrap are supported by npm normally. This PR makes the OpenClaw-owned plugin packages ship that lockfile too, and keeps plugin-only dependency graphs plugin-local instead of pretending the root package shrinkwrap covers them.

Because lockfiles can redirect resolved package versions and tarballs, this PR treats `pnpm-lock.yaml`, `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, and `package-lock.json` diffs as security-review surfaces. The package path rejects accidental `package-lock.json`, requires shrinkwrap for new root tarballs, and checks generated shrinkwraps have no dev dependency graph.

## Verification
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:generate`
- `pnpm deps:shrinkwrap:check`
- `bash scripts/plugin-npm-publish.sh --pack-dry-run extensions/amazon-bedrock | jq -r '.[0].files[].path' | grep '^npm-shrinkwrap.json$'`
- `NPM_CONFIG_CACHE=/tmp/openclaw-npm-cache npm pack --dry-run --ignore-scripts --json`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/scripts/dependency-change-awareness-workflow.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-changes-report.test.ts test/scripts/check-openclaw-package-tarball.test.ts test/openclaw-npm-release-check.test.ts test/package-scripts.test.ts test/package-manager-config.test.ts`
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs test/package-manager-config.test.ts test/plugin-npm-package-manifest.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-change-awareness-workflow.test.ts test/scripts/dependency-changes-report.test.ts test/package-scripts.test.ts`
- `pnpm check:docs`
- `git diff --check`
- `actionlint .github/workflows/dependency-change-awareness.yml .github/workflows/labeler.yml`
- `npm pack openclaw@2026.4.25 --json --pack-destination <tmp> && node scripts/check-openclaw-package-tarball.mjs <tmp>/*.tgz`

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: OpenClaw root npm installs and OpenClaw-owned npm plugin installs now carry publishable shrinkwrap files, while lockfile/shrinkwrap changes are treated as security-review surfaces.

Real environment tested: Local macOS checkout with root dry-run npm pack, package-local plugin pack dry-run, all generated shrinkwraps, focused Vitest coverage, docs checks, workflow linting, and a real published `openclaw@2026.4.25` tarball compatibility check.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: Generated and checked root plus 30 plugin shrinkwraps, verified `extensions/amazon-bedrock` pack dry-run includes `npm-shrinkwrap.json`, ran focused tests for release/tarball/dependency-review/plugin-package surfaces, docs check, actionlint, and legacy published tarball validation.

Evidence after fix: Root dry-run pack includes `npm-shrinkwrap.json` and excludes `package-lock.json`; generated plugin shrinkwraps exist for all 30 `publishToNpm` plugin packages; plugin shrinkwrap checks report current; selected generated shrinkwraps have 0 dev packages; Bedrock plugin pack dry-run emits `npm-shrinkwrap.json` in the packed file list.

Observed result after fix: Release/package checks and focused tests pass; the legacy published tarball emits a legacy shrinkwrap warning and passes; docs check passes; workflow lint and diff checks pass.

What was not tested: Full release CI, broad `pnpm check`, and an actual npm/plugin publish.

target.title: feat(comfy): dynamic aspect ratio → width/height injection
target.body:
## What Make the Comfy image generation tool respect `aspectRatio` and `size` parameters instead of ignoring them due to hard-coded dimensions in the workflow JSON. **This change also makes the `capabilities` config on all image providers dynamic**, not just Comfy. The core provider types, plugin-sdk exports, and image-generate tool were updated to support runtime capability resolution. ## Changes - **workflow-runtime.ts** - Added `ComfyDimensionConfig` type for target node configuration (`widthNodeId`, `heightNodeId`, input names) - Added `calculateDimensions()` — derives width/height from aspect ratio or size using a configurable base resolution (default 1024px), rounded to 64px increments per ComfyUI requirements - Added `setWorkflowDimensionInput()` — injects calculated dimensions into the workflow JSON at configured node(s) before submission - `runComfyWorkflow()` now accepts `aspectRatio` and `size` params - **image-generation-provider.ts** (Comfy) - Enabled `supportsAspectRatio: true` and `supportsSize: true` in generate capabilities - Passes both params through to `runComfyWorkflow()` - Capabilities are now resolved dynamically via `isComfyDimensionsConfigured()` - **openclaw.plugin.json** (Comfy) - Added `dimensions` config object under image capability schema (includes optional `baseSize`) - Made `widthNodeId`/`heightNodeId` required fields in the schema - **src/image-generation/types.ts** — all image providers affected - Added `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` type - `capabilities` field on `ImageGenerationProvider` is now `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities | ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver` (was just `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities`) - Added `resolveProviderCapabilities()` helper function - **src/plugin-sdk/image-generation.ts** — all providers affected - New exports: `resolveProviderCapabilities`, `ImageGenerationCapabilitiesResolver`, `ImageGenerationProviderCapabilities` - **src/agents/tools/image-generate-tool.ts** — all providers affected - Updated to use `resolveProviderCapabilities()` for capability checks - `validateImageGenerationCapabilities` now accepts optional `ctx` param for dynamic resolution - Both generate and edit path capability lookups go through the resolver ## Configuring Add a `dimensions` block to your Comfy image workflow config pointing at the node that controls output size: ```json "image": { "workflowPath": "...", "promptNodeId": "42", "dimensions": { "widthNodeId": "10", "heightNodeId": "10", "baseSize": 768 } } ``` - `widthNodeId` / `heightNodeId`: node IDs in your workflow JSON (can be the same node for joint width/height inputs) - `widthInputName` / `heightInputName`: input field names within those nodes (defaults: "width", "height") - `baseSize`: base resolution for aspect ratio calculation, defaults to 1024. Use 512 for SD 1.5, 768 for SDXL, etc. ## Backwards compatibility Falls back cleanly — no config or request changes needed: 1. No aspectRatio/size on request → dimension block skipped entirely 2. aspectRatio/size provided but no `dimensions` config → no injection, workflow unchanged 3. Invalid aspect ratio → `calculateDimensions()` returns null, no injection 4. Static `capabilities` object (no resolver function) → used as-is 5. `capabilities` resolved without context → same fallback behavior ## Tests - Explicit size injection into configured nodes - Calculated dimensions from aspect ratio (16:9 at base 1024 = 1024x576) - Size precedence over aspectRatio when both provided - Fallback when no dimensions config is present - Invalid aspect ratio gracefully ignored - Custom baseSize respected ## Real behavior proof
Chat log # Chat with Assistant ## You (2026-05-21T18:37:48.406Z) Draw a test image of a red circle, 640x960 dimensions. ## Assistant (2026-05-21T18:37:48.934Z) ## Tool (2026-05-21T18:38:18.118Z) Background task started for image generation (214a3d5b-9340-4b46-bcfe-0c704046755d). Do not call image_generate again for this request. Wait for the completion event; the completion agent will send the finished image here when it's ready. ## Assistant (2026-05-21T18:38:18.287Z) Image generation started — waiting for it to finish. 🎨 ## You (2026-05-21T18:39:31.578Z) [Inter-session message] sourceSession=image_generate:214a3d5b-9340-4b46-bcfe-0c704046755d sourceChannel=webchat sourceTool=image_generate isUser=false This content was routed by OpenClaw from another session or internal tool.
