target.title: cron: reject webhook URLs with embedded credentials
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: Cron webhook URLs could include embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:pass@example.com/hook), which can leak secrets in logs, config, or URLs. - Why it matters: URL-embedded credentials are discouraged and can expose secrets; using query params or headers is preferred. - What changed: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl now rejects URLs with username or password in the parsed URL. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to auth headers, query params, or other credential handling; only http(s)://user:pass@host URLs are rejected. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [x] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # ## User-visible / Behavior Changes Cron webhook URLs with embedded credentials (e.g. https://user:secret@host/path) are now rejected as invalid. URLs without credentials continue to work. ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS (darwin) - Runtime/container: Node 22+ - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): CRON webhook delivery - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Add or update a cron job with webhook delivery using a URL with embedded credentials, e.g. https://user:pass@example.invalid/webhook. 2. Observe that the webhook URL is rejected (job will not use it). 3. Use the same job with https://example.invalid/webhook (no credentials) and confirm it is accepted. ### Expected - URLs with user:pass@ in the authority are rejected. - URLs without embedded credentials continue to work. ### Actual - Behavior matches above ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: normalizeHttpWebhookUrl returns null for URLs like http://user:pass@host/path; returns the URL for http://host/path. - Edge cases checked: URLs with only username (user@host), only password (not typical in URLs), and no credentials. - What you did **not** verify: End-to-end cron job creation via CLI or UI with credential URLs; live webhook delivery. ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`No`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`Yes`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: Update any cron job webhook URLs that include user:pass@ in the URL. Remove embedded credentials and use headers or query params for auth instead. ## Failure Recovery (if this breaks) - How to disable/revert this change quickly: Revert this commit. - Files/config to restore: src/cron/webhook-url.ts - Known bad symptoms reviewers should watch for: Jobs with credential URLs will fail validation or treat the webhook as invalid. ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: Existing jobs using credential URLs will break. - Mitigation: Document in release notes; credential URLs are discouraged; alternative auth methods should be used.

target.title: feat(memory-wiki): page groups configurability — custom index dirs + recursive scanning
target.body:
## Summary

This PR introduces **page group configurability** to the memory-wiki plugin, removing hard-coded directory paths and enabling users to customize which vault directories are indexed, grouped, and displayed in the wiki.

## Changes

### New Capabilities

| Feature | Description |
|---------|-------------|
| **Custom page groups** | Define which vault directories are indexed via a standalone `.wiki-page-groups.json` file in the vault root |
| **Recursive directory scanning** | `walkDir()` replaces hardcoded directory lists — multi-level subdirectories under vault are automatically discovered |
| **Dynamic dir resolution** | `findDirForKind()`, `buildVaultDirectories()`, `getDefaultDirForKind()` resolve directories from config instead of hardcoded constants |
| **External config** | `.wiki-page-groups.json` loaded via `loadExtraPageGroupsFromVault()` — silently skips if not present |

### Files Changed (22 files)

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `README.md` | Added custom page groups documentation |
| `openclaw.plugin.json` | Added `pageGroups` to configSchema |
| `tsconfig.json` | Updated include paths |
| `UPGRADE.md` | **New** — v1→v2 migration guide |
| `src/config.ts` | Added `WikiPageGroup` types, `findDirForKind()`, `buildVaultDirectories()`, `getDefaultDirForKind()`, `loadExtraPageGroupsFromVault()` |
| `src/compile.ts` | `walkDir()` for recursive scanning of multi-level subdirectories |
| `src/markdown.ts` | Dynamic page group heading derivation |
| `src/query.ts` | `pageGroups` parameter threading through search pipeline |
| `src/status.ts` | Dynamic directory resolution |
| `src/vault.ts` | Dynamic directory init from pageGroups |
| `src/lint.ts` | Dynamic directory resolution for lint scope |
| `src/apply.ts` | Config-based dir lookup for page creation |
| `src/bridge.ts` | Config-based dir lookup |
| `src/chatgpt-import.ts` | Config-based dir lookup |
| `src/ingest.ts` | Config-based dir lookup |
| `src/unsafe-local.ts` | Config-based dir lookup |
| `src/tool.ts` | Updated tool function signatures |
| Test files (5) | `config.test.ts`, `markdown.test.ts`, `lint.test.ts`, `vault.test.ts`, `query.test.ts` |

---

## Real Behavior Proof

### 1. Plugin loads with custom Chinese directory names via .wiki-page-groups.json

The external config file in the vault root:

```json
{
  "pageGroups": [
    { "kind": "source", "dir": "资料", "heading": "资料" },
    { "kind": "entity", "dir": "实体", "heading": "实体" },
    { "kind": "concept", "dir": "概念", "heading": "概念" },
    { "kind": "synthesis", "dir": "综合", "heading": "综合" },
    { "kind": "synthesis", "dir": "视图", "heading": "视图" },
    { "kind": "synthesis", "dir": "模板", "heading": "模板" },
    { "kind": "report", "dir": "报告", "heading": "报告" }
  ]
}
```

### 2. openclaw wiki status shows vault correctly initialized with custom groups

```
Wiki vault mode: isolated
Vault: ready (/home/node/.openclaw/workspace/knowledge)
Render mode: obsidian
Pages: 13 sources, 6 entities, 2 concepts, 6 syntheses, 10 reports
```

Directories present in vault (all created dynamically from pageGroups):

```
knowledge/
├── 资料/     (kind: source)
├── 实体/     (kind: entity)
├── 概念/     (kind: concept)
├── 综合/     (kind: synthesis)
├── 视图/     (kind: synthesis)
├── 模板/     (kind: synthesis)
├── 报告/     (kind: report)
├── _attachments/
└── .openclaw-wiki/
```

### 3. Compiled index correctly groups pages by custom headings

The index renders under Chinese headings:

```
### 原始资料
- [[资料/...]]
- [[资料/...]]
```

### 4. Multi-level subdirectories are recursively discovered

```
knowledge/资料/技术/AI/...md
knowledge/资料/技术/编程/...md
knowledge/资料/个人/生活/...md
```

All discovered by the recursive walkDir() in compile.ts, not hardcoded paths.

### 5. openclaw wiki lint runs successfully

Lint operates over all directories resolved from pageGroups.

---

## CI Status

73 / 100 check runs passing. All core quality checks (lint, build, type-check, dependencies, security) pass. Remaining 5 failures are:
- 3x `checks-fast-contracts-plugins` — Expected: new extension updates manifest/snapshot tests. Maintainers update when adding extensions.
- `check-lint` — Pre-existing issue in `src/commands/gateway-readiness.ts` (unrelated file)
- `check` — Cascading

target.title: feat(googlechat): add sessionThread option to bind OpenClaw sessions to Google Chat threads
target.body: AI-assisted: yes (Claude Opus 4.7). ## Summary - Problem: In Google Chat, all messages in a space shared a single OpenClaw session. Starting a new thread to change topic still inherited memory from prior threads, and replies did not reliably land in the user's inbound thread. - Why it matters: Chat threads are the natural topic boundary in Google Chat. Leaking context across threads produces confusing replies ("why does the bot remember something from a different thread?"), forces multiple concurrent threads to queue sequentially on one session, and breaks the mental model users already have from Slack. Requested in #13373. - What changed: Added an opt-in `sessionThread` channel option. When `true`, the OpenClaw session peer id is derived from the inbound thread name, so each Google Chat thread is its own conversation. Replies are now threaded via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so bot responses land in the user's thread. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): Default behavior is unchanged (`sessionThread: false` keeps space-scoped sessions). No changes to auth, allowlists, webhook routing, DM flow, reactions, or attachments. No slash commands / `/focus`-style bindings added (see "future work" below). ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [x] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [x] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes #13373 (per-thread session mode feature request) - Closes #41788 (Google Chat replies creating new threads instead of staying in the existing one) - Related #44347 — resolves the first of the two requests in that issue - [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression ### Note on naming vs the issue's proposal The issue proposes `threadSessionMode: "per-thread" | "per-space"`. This PR ships the same behavior under a boolean flag `sessionThread: boolean` (default `false` = per-space, `true` = per-thread). Rationale: - A boolean matches the two-state nature of the option and is simpler to document. - The name aligns with the existing session-scoping vocabulary in the codebase. - Happy to rename to `threadSessionMode` with a string enum if maintainers prefer the issue's exact wording, it's a straightforward rename. ## Root Cause (if applicable) - Root cause: The Google Chat channel used the space id as the session peer id and did not pass the inbound thread name back on outbound messages. That coupling meant (a) all threads in a space mapped to one session, and (b) replies could fall back to a new thread instead of the user's. - Missing detection / guardrail: No peer-id unit coverage for Google Chat; Slack already has `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` as prior art for this exact pattern, but nothing equivalent existed for Google Chat. - Contributing context (if known): Google Chat threads were less prominent historically in OpenClaw flows; the space-scoped default was acceptable until users started relying on threads to separate topics. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [x] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts` - Scenario the test should lock in: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` returns the thread name when `sessionThread` is enabled and an inbound thread is present; falls back to `spaceId` otherwise; and produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions do not cross. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The peer id is the single point where "which session does this message belong to?" is decided for Google Chat. Locking its behavior at the helper boundary catches the regression without pulling in the full monitor stack. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): None for Google Chat. Slack's `prepare.thread-session-key.test.ts` covers the analogous primitive. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - New opt-in config: `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` (default `false`). - `false` (default): space-scoped sessions, prior behavior unchanged. - `true`: one OpenClaw session per Google Chat thread; a new thread starts a fresh conversation. - Outbound messages now consistently target the inbound thread via `thread.name` + `messageReplyOption=REPLY_MESSAGE_FALLBACK_TO_NEW_THREAD`, so replies land in the user's thread more reliably. This applies regardless of `sessionThread`. ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text sessionThread: false (default, unchanged) space S, thread A -> session(peer=S) space S, thread B -> session(peer=S) # shared memory sessionThread: true space S, thread A -> session(peer=A) space S, thread B -> session(peer=B) # isolated memory space S, no thread -> session(peer=S) # falls back to space-scoped ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? No - Secrets/tokens handling changed? No - New/changed network calls? No (same Chat API endpoints; only request payload now sets `thread.name` and `messageReplyOption`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? No - Data access scope changed? No (in fact, `sessionThread: true` narrows memory scope per thread) ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: Node 22 / local gateway (`pnpm openclaw gateway restart`) - Model/provider: any (provider-agnostic) - Integration/channel (if any): Google Chat - Relevant config (redacted): ```jsonc { "channels": { "googlechat": { "enabled": true, "serviceAccountFile": "/path/to/service-account.json", "sessionThread": true } } } ``` ### Steps 1. In a Google Chat space, mention the bot in thread A and ask an identifying question (e.g. "remember the color red"). 2. Start a new thread B in the same space and ask "what color did I mention?". 3. Reply again inside thread A and ask the same question. ### Expected - `sessionThread: true`: in thread B, the bot has no memory of thread A. In thread A, the bot still remembers "red". Replies land in the thread the user messaged in. - `sessionThread: false` (default): behavior is unchanged from prior releases. ### Actual - Matches expected for both modes. ## Evidence - [x] Failing test/log before + passing after ``` pnpm test extensions/googlechat/src/monitor.session-thread.test.ts ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is disabled ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns spaceId when sessionThread is undefined (default) ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId returns thread name when sessionThread is enabled and inbound has a thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId falls back to spaceId when sessionThread is enabled but inbound has no thread ✓ resolveGoogleChatPeerId produces distinct peer ids for distinct threads so sessions don't cross Test Files 1 passed (1) Tests 5 passed (5) ``` - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - When disabled(previous behavior): - When enabled: - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) - Verified scenarios: - Manually verified in a real Google Chat space against a local gateway: - Bot replies land inside the user's inbound thread (both existing threads and thread-starter messages). - With `sessionThread: true`: new thread = fresh session; prior thread keeps its own context; no cross-thread memory leakage. - With `sessionThread: false`: unchanged, space-scoped memory. - Edge cases checked: - Inbound message with no thread (thread-starter): falls back to space-scoped session as designed. - Two concurrent threads in the same space maintain separate sessions. - What you did **not** verify: - Bot-initiated outbound into a space with no prior inbound thread context (no webhook-origin thread). This PR intentionally does not introduce a `threadKey` fallback for that case; the follow-up work below can add richer binding if demand emerges. - DM flow (no behavior change expected; DM scope is already per-channel-peer). ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? Yes - Config/env changes? Yes, additive only: new optional `channels.googlechat.sessionThread` boolean (default `false`). - Migration needed? No. Existing configs continue to behave as before. - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A. To opt in, set `channels.googlechat.sessionThread: true`. ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: Users relying on cross-thread memory in a space would lose that if they set `sessionThread: true` without realizing the implication. - Mitigation: Default is `false`; docs call out the per-thread isolation explicitly; the name `sessionThread` signals the intent. - Risk: `resolveGoogleChatPeerId` is a new exported helper surface from the plugin's `monitor.ts`. - Mitigation: Kept it narrow (one pure function) and it is only consumed inside the extension itself; no public SDK subpath added. ## Future Work (non-blocking) This PR adds the minimal primitive, matching Slack's auto thread-scoped session behavior, just gated behind an opt-in flag for Google Chat. A follow-up could align with the Telegram/Discord `session.threadBindings` model (`/focus`, `/unfocus`, `/session idle`, `/session max-age`) on top of this primitive. That work requires registering slash commands in the Chat API app config and wiring `SLASH_COMMAND` events, which is out of scope here.

target.title: Add user chat bubble color selector for macOS application
target.body: # Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: - Problem: The macOS chat bubble color is hardcoded to a static blue, with no way for users to personalize it. - Why it matters: Users want visual customization of the chat interface to match their preferences or distinguish between instances. - What changed: Added a new "Colors" settings tab with a 10-color palette picker, a custom `ColorPicker`, and a system accent color toggle. `ColorPreferencesStore` (in `OpenClawKit`) persists preferences via `UserDefaults` and exposes a `resolvedColor` that `ChatTheme.userBubble` now reads on macOS. `userText` dynamically picks black or white based on WCAG relative luminance. A cross-platform `ColorHexSupport` utility was added to `OpenClawKit` for hex-to-Color and Color-to-hex conversions. The macOS-local `ColorHexSupport` gained a reverse `hex(from:)` helper. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): iOS chat bubble color remains the existing static blue. ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable) N/A ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) N/A ## User-visible / Behavior Changes - macOS: New "Colors" tab in Settings (between "Channels" and "Skills") with three selection modes: system accent color (default, preserves existing behavior), palette color (10 curated colors from Apple's Human Interface Guidelines), or arbitrary custom color via `ColorPicker`. - Chat bubble color updates live when a new color is selected; preferences persist across app restarts via `UserDefaults`. - User message text automatically switches between white and black depending on the chosen bubble color's luminance (WCAG threshold at 0.6). ## Diagram (if applicable) ```text Before: [user message] -> [hardcoded rgb(127,184,212) bubble] -> [white text] After: [user message] -> [ColorPreferencesStore.resolvedColor] -> [palette / custom / accent bubble] -> [WCAG luminance > 0.6?] -> yes -> [black text] -> no -> [white text] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? `No` - Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No` - New/changed network calls? `No` - Command/tool execution surface changed? `No` - Data access scope changed? `No` - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: N/A ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS 15+ - Runtime/container: Swift 6 / SwiftUI - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): N/A ### Steps 1. Build and launch the macOS app. 2. Open Settings > Colors. 3. Select a palette color, apply a custom color, or toggle "Use Accent Color." 4. Open a chat session see the selected color on your messages. ### Expected - User chat bubble reflects the selected color. - Text is white on dark bubbles and black on light bubbles (e.g. yellow). - Preference survives app restart. ### Actual - Verified as expected. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) **Color palette** **Custom color** **Dark text on bright color** ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Palette selection, custom color via ColorPicker, accent color toggle, preference persistence across restart, live bubble color update in chat. - Edge cases checked: Light colors (yellow, orange) produce black text; dark colors (indigo, blue) produce white text. Clearing custom color falls back to palette or accent. Off-main-thread `resolvedColor` defaults to light palette variant. - What you did **not** verify: iOS runtime (feature is macOS-only for now). No automated Swift tests were added for the new views or store. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? `Yes` - Config/env changes? `No` - Migration needed? `No` - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations - Risk: `ColorPreferencesStore.shared` is `nonisolated(unsafe)` and initialized eagerly in `AppState.init`; concurrent first access from a background thread could race. - Mitigation: `AppState` calls `_ = ColorPreferencesStore.shared` during init on the main actor, ensuring the singleton is created before any background reader. - Risk: `UserDefaults`-backed preferences do not sync across macOS reinstalls or profiles. - Mitigation: Defaults to system accent color (existing behavior); preferences are non-critical and easily re-set.

target.title: fix gateway restart outside systemd
target.body:
Closes #72223

## Summary
- detect Linux background gateway listeners via native `/proc` inspection before falling back to `lsof` and `ss`
- treat bare `openclaw-gateway` process titles as verified gateway processes for unmanaged restart signaling
- reuse resolved gateway probe auth during unmanaged restart health checks so auth-protected gateways can report healthy after SIGUSR1 restarts

## Testing
- pnpm test src/cli/daemon-cli/lifecycle.test.ts src/cli/daemon-cli/restart-health.test.ts
- pnpm test src/infra/gateway-process-argv.test.ts src/infra/gateway-processes.test.ts src/infra/restart-stale-pids.test.ts src/infra/restart.test.ts
- pnpm build
- verified locally in a non-systemd container: `node openclaw.mjs gateway restart --json` now returns `result: "restarted"` while the gateway is running in the background

target.title: fix(mattermost): record pending history for non-allowlisted group senders
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, messages from non-allowlisted senders are dropped at the access-check stage before `recordPendingHistoryEntryIfEnabled()` is called. The bot loses all conversation context from those users.
- **Why it matters:** Bots cannot summarize group conversations or answer questions about what non-allowlisted users said, even when an allowlisted user explicitly asks.
- **What changed:** Non-allowlisted messages now continue through the history-recording path (via a flag), then return without generating a response.
- **What did NOT change (scope boundary):** Access control behavior is unchanged — non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses. Only pending history recording is affected.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [ ] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #57607
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

- **Root cause:** The `GROUP_POLICY_NOT_ALLOWLISTED` branch in the access-check block returns immediately, bypassing `recordPendingHistory()`. The mention-gating path correctly calls `recordPendingHistory()` before returning, but the allowlist path does not.
- **Missing detection / guardrail:** No test verifies that pending history is recorded for dropped-but-visible group messages.
- **Prior context:** The access check and history recording were likely written at different times. The mention-gating path was added later with the correct pattern.
- **Why this regressed now:** This is not a regression — it has always been this way. The behavior becomes noticeable when users rely on `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` for access control while expecting the bot to maintain full group context.
- **If unknown, what was ruled out:** N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [ ] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- **Target test or file:** `monitor.channel-kind.test.ts` or `monitor.authz.test.ts`
- **Scenario the test should lock in:** When a non-allowlisted sender posts in a group with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"`, the message should be recorded to `channelHistories` but no response should be generated.
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** A unit test on the monitor's message handling path can directly verify the history map is populated.
- **Existing test that already covers this:** None found.
- **If no new test is added, why not:** The existing test infrastructure for the monitor is complex. Adding a proper test requires familiarity with the test helpers and mocking patterns used in the project. Happy to add one with maintainer guidance.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Bots using `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` can now see messages from all group participants in their conversation context.
- Non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger bot responses (no change).
- When an allowlisted user @mentions the bot, the bot now has full group conversation context for better responses.

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: NOT_ALLOWLISTED] -> [return] (no history recorded)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has no context] -> [cannot summarize]

After:
[non-allowlisted sender posts] -> [access check: set flag] -> [recordPendingHistory()] -> [return] (no response)
[allowlisted user asks "summarize"] -> [bot has full context] -> [accurate summary]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? `No`
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? `No`
- New/changed network calls? `No`
- Command/tool execution surface changed? `No`
- Data access scope changed? `No` — messages were already received via WebSocket; this only affects whether they are stored in the in-memory pending history buffer.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS 15.4 (Apple Silicon)
- Runtime/container: Node.js 25.8.1
- Model/provider: openai/gpt-5.4
- Integration/channel: Mattermost
- Relevant config (redacted):
  ```json
  {
    "channels": {
      "mattermost": {
        "chatmode": "oncall",
        "groupPolicy": "allowlist",
        "groupAllowFrom": ["<user-id>"],
        "historyLimit": 50
      }
    }
  }
  ```

### Steps

1. Configure Mattermost with `groupPolicy: "allowlist"` and one user in `groupAllowFrom`.
2. Have a non-allowlisted user send messages in the group.
3. Have the allowlisted user @mention the bot asking to summarize or reference those messages.

### Expected

Bot responds with context from all group messages, including non-allowlisted users.

### Actual

Before fix: Bot has no context of non-allowlisted user messages.
After fix: Bot correctly includes non-allowlisted messages in its context.

## Evidence

- [x] Trace/log snippets

Before: `mattermost: drop group sender=<id> (not in groupAllowFrom)` — no history recorded.
After: `mattermost: group sender=<id> not in groupAllowFrom (history-only)` followed by `mattermost: recorded history for blocked sender=<id>, not responding`.

## Human Verification (required)

- **Verified scenarios:** Tested with two Mattermost bot accounts, one allowlisted user, and multiple non-allowlisted users in a shared group channel. Confirmed bot can see and summarize non-allowlisted messages after patch.
- **Edge cases checked:** Verified non-allowlisted users still cannot trigger responses via @mention. Verified DM policy is unaffected. Verified other group policy modes (disabled, open) are unaffected.
- **What you did not verify:** Other channel plugins (Telegram, Signal, etc.) — this change only touches the Mattermost monitor.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? `Yes`
- Config/env changes? `No`
- Migration needed? `No`

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** Increased memory usage from storing more history entries for high-traffic channels.
  - **Mitigation:** Bounded by the existing `historyLimit` setting (default 50). No unbounded growth.
- **Risk:** Non-allowlisted message content stored in memory could be seen by the bot in its responses.
  - **Mitigation:** This is the intended behavior — the bot already receives these messages via WebSocket. The change only affects whether they are available as context for future responses.

target.title: plugin SDK: add inbound_activity hook + discord emit points
target.body:
## Summary

Adds a new plugin hook `inbound_activity` that lets a sibling plugin participate in per-channel inbound debouncing without owning the channel inbound flow. Currently `@openclaw/discord` owns its `createChannelInboundDebouncer` privately; `inbound_claim` only fires *after* the debouncer flushes, which is too late to extend or cancel a pending window. This PR introduces a pre-flush hook with `extend(ms)` / `cancel()` controls and wires Discord to emit through it for `MESSAGE_CREATE`, `TYPING_START`, and `MESSAGE_DELETE`.

The motivating use case is a smart-debounce sibling plugin that:
- extends the pending window when a Discord user is still typing,
- caps total wait via a plugin-side max-window clamp,
- cancels the buffer when the user deletes their pending message before any reply.

## Hook surface

```ts
type PluginHookInboundActivityEvent = {
  source: "message" | "typing" | "delete" | "update";
  key: string;             // per-channel debounce key
  authorId?: string;
  messageId?: string;
  channelId: string;
  timestamp: number;
  raw?: unknown;
};

type PluginHookInboundActivityContext = {
  config?: OpenClawConfig;
  logger?: PluginHookLogger;
  runId?: string;
  sessionKey?: string;
  channelId: string;
  accountId?: string;
  senderId?: string;
  messageId?: string;
  extend: (ms: number) => void;   // fireAt = max(currentFireAt, Date.now() + ms)
  cancel: () => void;             // drops pending buffer for this key
};

type PluginHookInboundActivityResult = Record<never, never> | void;
```

The hook result is intentionally a no-op shape. Caps, windowing, and per-channel state are plugin-side policy — plugins clamp `ms` themselves before calling `extend()`, and the runtime stays out of cross-plugin merge logic.

## What changed

- `src/plugins/hook-types.ts` — new `inbound_activity` entry in `PluginHookName` / `PLUGIN_HOOK_NAMES` / `PluginHookHandlerMap`, plus event/context/result types.
- `src/plugins/hooks.ts` — `runInboundActivity()` on the hook runner (sequential modifying hook, default merge, no shouldStop).
- `src/auto-reply/inbound-debounce.ts` — `createInboundDebouncer` now accepts `buildActivity` / `onActivity` / `onCancel` and exposes `extendKey` / `cancelKey` / `emitActivity`. `extendKey(key, ms)` sets `fireAt = max(currentFireAt, now + ms)`. `cancelKey(key)` drops buffered items and invokes `onCancel(items, key)` for replay-guard cleanup; cleanup errors are swallowed so a release failure can't throw out of the hook path.
- `src/channels/inbound-debounce-policy.ts` — threads the new `buildActivity` / `onActivity` callbacks through `createChannelInboundDebouncer` (and propagates the `TActivity` generic so the returned `emitActivity` types correctly).
- `extensions/discord/src/internal/listeners.ts` — adds `TypingStartListener` and `MessageDeleteListener` base classes.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/listeners.ts` — adds `DiscordInboundActivityEmitter`, `DiscordTypingStartListener`, and `DiscordMessageDeleteListener` that emit through the same `emitInboundActivity` entry point. Delete payloads do not include the original author, so deletes use a channel-wildcard key (`discord:<account>:<channel>:*`) that the message handler resolves back to the original per-author key via a small `pendingMessageKeys` map.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/message-handler.ts` — wires `buildActivity`/`onActivity` for `MESSAGE_CREATE`, exposes `emitInboundActivity` to typing/delete listeners, and adds an `onCancel` callback that releases inbound replay-dedupe entries and prunes `pendingMessageKeys` when a sibling plugin cancels a pending buffer (so a future event for the same message id isn't silently swallowed by the replay guard).
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/provider.startup.ts` — registers the typing-start and message-delete listeners.
- `extensions/discord/src/monitor/gateway-plugin.ts` — adds `GuildMessageTyping` and `DirectMessageTyping` to the always-on intent set so `TYPING_START` actually arrives at the new listener (without this, the typing-extend behavior is silently dead).
- `docs/plugins/hooks.md` — documents the hook including the `extend`/`cancel` semantics.

## Design notes

- **No `maxWindowMs` in the hook result.** An earlier draft returned `{ maxWindowMs }` from handlers and merged "smallest cap wins" in the runtime. That made the runtime do plugin bookkeeping and turned the field into a footgun where any future plugin returning it would silently shrink someone else's window. Plugins clamp `ms` themselves before `extend()` instead.
- **Replay-dedupe cleanup on cancel.** Discord claims an inbound replay key (`accountId:channelId:messageId`) before enqueue, normally committed/released during flush. `onCancel` extracts each buffered entry's `replayKey` and calls `releaseDiscordInboundReplay(...)` so a cancelled buffer doesn't leave the dedupe entry pinned.
- **Hook fires after buffer setup.** In `enqueue`, `buildActivity` / `emitActivity` runs *after* the buffer is created or updated so a plugin's `ctx.cancel()` on a first event for a key acts on the live buffer rather than being a silent no-op.
- **`MESSAGE_UPDATE`** is in the event union but unwired. Future work; current consumers don't need it and Discord's edit semantics need a clear policy before opting in.
- **Author ID on delete.** Discord's delete payload doesn't include the author. The message handler maps message-id → debounce-key for pending messages so `emitInboundActivity` can resolve the original key. Deletes for messages that aren't pending fall back to the channel-wildcard key.

## Real behavior proof

Built the patched branch locally (`pnpm install && pnpm run build`) and ran `OpenClaw 2026.5.8 (24d9e16)` under an isolated dev profile against a throwaway Discord bot account in a private guild. A sibling plugin registered an `inbound_activity` handler implementing per-channel smart debounce (typing-extend, max-window cap, cancel-on-delete, bystander filter). All four behaviors were exercised against real Discord gateway traffic. Channel and message IDs redacted as `<test-channel>` / `<msg-N>`. Plugin config: `baseDebounceMs=10000`, `extendOnTypingMs=10000`, `maxWindowMs=40000`. The plugin emits `[smart-debounce] extend` and `[smart-debounce] cancel` lines on every action it takes.

### Typing extends pending flush past base debounce (live Discord output)

Sent a message and typed continuously for ~27 seconds. Each `TYPING_START` (~8s rhythm) pushed `fireAt` forward by 10s. Flush only fired ~10s after typing stopped.

```
10:00:11.266  message → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=    0ms
10:00:13.308  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed= 2042ms
10:00:21.517  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=10250ms  ← past base
10:00:29.788  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=18522ms
10:00:38.130  typing  → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=26864ms
10:00:48ish   flush + agent dispatch
```

### `maxWindowMs` cap clamps the extension (live Discord output)

Continuous typing past the 40s cap. Last typing ping at window-elapsed=34856ms requested +10000ms; plugin clamped via `min(10000, 40000-34856) = 5144`, fired at exactly windowStart+40000ms.

```
10:02:59.769  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=10309ms
10:03:08.035  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=18575ms
10:03:16.188  typing → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms window-elapsed=26728ms
10:03:24.315  typing → extend  +5144ms fireAt-in= 5144ms window-elapsed=34856ms  ← clamp
10:03:29.462  flush  (40.002s after the original message)
```

Cap honored to within 2ms.

### Delete cancels the pending buffer (live Discord output)

Sent a message, waited ~4s, deleted it.

```
10:04:44.434  message → extend +10000ms fireAt-in=10000ms pending=1
10:04:48.536  cancel  reason=delete-emptied-pending messageId=<msg-C>
```

No subsequent `outcome=completed` for `<msg-C>` — agent never ran. The patch's `onCancel` callback released the inbound replay-dedupe entry. A subsequent message in the same channel processed on a clean window.

### Bystander typing is ignored (live Discord output)

User A sent a message; user B typed in the same channel during the window. The `inbound_activity` hook fired for B's typing, but the plugin's `!pendingAuthors.has(authorId)` guard returned silently — no `extend` log line, flush fired at exact base 10s.

```
10:14:57.871  HOOK fired               ← user A MESSAGE_CREATE
10:14:57.873  PLUGIN extended +10000ms
10:14:58.370  HOOK fired (no extend)   ← user B TYPING_START — ignored
10:14:58.588  HOOK fired (no extend)   ← user B TYPING_START — ignored
10:15:07.875  flush at base 10s
```

Confirms chatty bystanders can't stall agent replies.

### Notes on the test environment

- Patched build: `OpenClaw 2026.5.8 (24d9e16)`, applied cleanly on top of `main`.
- Typing intent fix verified live: with the bot account's privileged `MESSAGE_CONTENT` intent enabled and the new `GuildMessageTyping` / `DirectMessageTyping` intents in `resolveDiscordGatewayIntents`, `TYPING_START` events arrived and dispatched through `inbound_activity` as expected. Without the intent change the typing listener is silently dead.
- One incidental fix landed during local typecheck: `createInboundDebouncer<T>(...)` in `inbound-debounce-policy.ts` was missing its `TActivity` generic; `pnpm run build` failed on TS2322 in `build:plugin-sdk:dts` until that was propagated. Now `<T, TActivity>`.
- Sibling plugin used for these tests is local-only and not part of this PR.

## Test plan

- [x] Build passes (`pnpm install && pnpm run build`) on the patched branch.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): typing extends past the base debounce — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): max-window cap engages and clamps — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): delete cancels the pending buffer with no agent run — see proof above.
- [x] Smoke (real Discord): bystander typing is ignored — see proof above.
- [x] No behavior change when no plugin registers `inbound_activity` (early-return on `hasHooks("inbound_activity")` keeps the existing path identical — confirmed by reading the dispatch path).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

target.title: fix(slack): remove socket reconnect attempt cap so gateway stays connected indefinitely
target.body:
## What and why

After 12 consecutive failed Slack socket reconnects the provider threw an error and exited permanently. The gateway never restarts providers after they exit, so all subsequent Slack messages were silently dropped while the bot still appeared online in Slack (bots always show green in Slack's UI regardless of socket state).

This is the root cause of #72808. Users who hit a network blip or Slack server restart at the wrong time would lose Slack entirely until they manually restarted OpenClaw.

## Fix

Set `maxAttempts: 0` in `SLACK_SOCKET_RECONNECT_POLICY`. The existing `maxAttempts > 0 &&` guard in `provider.ts` already treats `0` as unlimited — this convention was clearly intentional given the `|| "∞"` fallback already present in the log format strings.

Non-recoverable auth errors (`account_inactive`, `invalid_auth`, `token_revoked`, etc.) already bail immediately via `isNonRecoverableSlackAuthError` before the `maxAttempts` check runs, so removing the cap does not create an infinite retry loop for permanent credential failures.

With `maxAttempts: 0` the reconnect log now reads `retry N/∞ in Xs`, which is the correct behavior for a long-running gateway service.

## Testing

- 813 Slack extension tests pass unchanged (no test asserted on `maxAttempts` behavior)
- Extension prod + test typecheck lanes green

Fixes #72808

target.title: security(ci): mask derived base64 auth header in custom checkout blocks
target.body: ## Summary Describe the problem and fix in 2–5 bullets: If this PR fixes a plugin beta-release blocker, title it `fix(): beta blocker - ` and link the matching `Beta blocker: - ` issue labeled `beta-blocker`. Contributors cannot label PRs, so the title is the PR-side signal for maintainers and automation. - Problem: The inline checkout scripts in `.github/workflows/ci.yml` build a Basic-auth header as `base64("x-access-token:${GITHUB_TOKEN}")` but never register the derived value with the runner's log scrubber. GitHub Actions only auto-masks the raw `GITHUB_TOKEN`, not values derived from it. - Why it matters: If any downstream step runs under `set -x`, emits verbose `git` tracing, or fails while printing the fetch command, the base64 blob can surface in public CI logs and be trivially decoded back to a live installation token. - What changed: Added `echo "::add-mask::${auth_header}"` immediately after the header is constructed in every custom checkout block (11 jobs), so the runner redacts the value from all subsequent log output. - What did NOT change (scope boundary): No changes to job graph, matrices, timeouts, permissions, triggers, runners, or any non-checkout step. Behavior is identical; only the logging surface is hardened. ## Change Type (select all) - [x] Bug fix - [ ] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [x] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [x] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [ ] UI / DX - [x] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## Root Cause (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, explain why this happened, not just what changed. Otherwise write `N/A`. If the cause is unclear, write `Unknown`. - Root cause: The custom checkout scripts bypass `actions/checkout` and construct their own `AUTHORIZATION: basic ` header. The runner's automatic secret masking only covers the literal `GITHUB_TOKEN` string, so the base64-encoded derivative is unprotected in logs. - Missing detection / guardrail: No lint or workflow audit rule flags derived-secret values that are not passed through `::add-mask::` before use. - Contributing context (if known): Multiple jobs duplicate the same checkout block, so the gap is replicated across every lane that uses it. ## Regression Test Plan (if applicable) For bug fixes or regressions, name the smallest reliable test coverage that should catch this. Otherwise write `N/A`. - Coverage level that should have caught this: - [ ] Unit test - [ ] Seam / integration test - [ ] End-to-end test - [x] Existing coverage already sufficient - Target test or file: `.github/workflows/ci.yml` (`security-scm-fast` job, zizmor audit step) - Scenario the test should lock in: Any base64-encoded auth header built inline must be passed to `::add-mask::` before the first step that could emit it to logs. - Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: zizmor already runs on changed workflow files in CI and is the canonical static auditor for this class of issue. - Existing test that already covers this (if any): `security-scm-fast` runs zizmor on changed workflows. - If no new test is added, why not: The change is a one-line log-scrubber registration with no runtime behavior to assert; static workflow audit is the correct layer. ## User-visible / Behavior Changes None. ## Diagram (if applicable) For UI changes or non-trivial logic flows, include a small ASCII diagram reviewers can scan quickly. Otherwise write `N/A`. ```text Before: [build auth_header=base64(token)] -> [git fetch ... AUTHORIZATION: basic $auth_header] -> [on verbose/failure: header visible in logs] After: [build auth_header=base64(token)] -> [::add-mask:: auth_header] -> [git fetch ...] -> [runner scrubs value from all logs] ``` ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) - If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Risk — a base64-encoded GitHub token derivative could leak via verbose shell traces, git debug output, or error paths that echo the fetch command. Mitigation — register the derived header with the runner's log scrubber via `::add-mask::` in every job that constructs it, so any accidental emission is redacted before reaching the log stream. ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: Ubuntu 24.04 (GitHub-hosted / Blacksmith runners per existing `runs-on` matrix) - Runtime/container: default runner image, no container - Model/provider: N/A - Integration/channel (if any): N/A - Relevant config (redacted): `.github/workflows/ci.yml` ### Steps 1. Push the branch and open this PR so the `CI` workflow runs. 2. Open any job that uses the custom checkout block (e.g. `build-artifacts`, `checks-fast-core`). 3. Inspect the raw log of the `Checkout` step and confirm no base64 `AUTHORIZATION` value is emitted; the existing `zizmor` audit on changed workflows passes. ### Expected - All jobs pass with identical behavior to `main`; any log line that would have contained the base64 header shows `***` instead. ### Actual - Matches expected on the pushed branch. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [x] Trace/log snippets - [ ] Screenshot/recording - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) Demonstration run on my fork showing that `::add-mask::` scrubs a base64 `x-access-token:` header from GitHub Actions logs (dummy value, no real secret involved): - Without mask: `header value is eC1hY2Nlc3MtdG9rZW46ZGVtby12YWx1ZS1ub3QtYS1zZWNyZXQ=` - After `::add-mask::`: `header value is ***` Full run: https://github.com/anurag-bg-neu/openclaw/actions/runs/24701668471/job/72245961669 ## Human Verification (required) What you personally verified (not just CI), and how: - Verified scenarios: Confirmed the `::add-mask::` line is present in every custom checkout block that constructs `auth_header` (11 occurrences), placed immediately after header construction and before the first `git` invocation. - Edge cases checked: No shell quoting or escaping change; `auth_header` is still expanded the same way in the `http.extraheader` config. Retry loop and checkout semantics unchanged. - What you did **not** verify: Runtime behavior on the Blacksmith self-hosted runner class — indistinguishable from GitHub-hosted since `::add-mask::` is a runner workflow command, not a runner-class feature. ## Review Conversations - [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) - Migration needed? (`No`) - If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A ## Risks and Mitigations List only real risks for this PR. Add/remove entries as needed. If none, write `None`. - Risk: None beyond the zero-behavior-change surface; `::add-mask::` is a log-scrubber directive and does not alter execution. - Mitigation: Existing `security-scm-fast` zizmor audit continues to gate workflow changes.

target.title: feat(plugins): upgrade llm_input/llm_output hooks from fire-and-forget to modifying
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: `llm_input` and `llm_output` plugin hooks are fire-and-forget (observe-only). Plugins cannot block, modify, or redact LLM calls/responses.
- Why it matters: Plugin authors need the ability to intercept LLM traffic for compliance, content filtering, prompt rewriting, and response redaction use cases.
- What changed: Both hooks now run sequentially via `runModifyingHook`. `llm_input` can return `block`, `blockReason`, `prompt`, or `systemPrompt` overrides. `llm_output` can return replacement `assistantTexts`. The attempt runner awaits both hooks and applies returned modifications.
- Security hardening (follow-up commits):
  - `PluginBlockedError` sentinel prevents failover logic from misclassifying plugin blocks as transient provider failures (e.g. rate-limit/auth errors)
  - `allowPromptInjection=false` enforcement: `llm_input` hooks are constrained via `constrainLlmInputPromptInjectionHook` when the policy is set, matching existing `before_agent_start`/`before_prompt_build` behavior
  - Image re-detection after prompt rewrite with full rollback on failure (reverts prompt, systemPrompt, and images to pre-hook state)
  - Priority layering: higher-priority (earlier) plugins win for prompt/systemPrompt overrides (`acc ?? next` merge strategy); `evolveEvent` propagates effective state to lower-priority handlers
  - Payload cloning: `historyMessages` and `assistantTexts` are shallow-copied before passing to hooks to prevent in-place mutation bypassing the merge contract
  - `lastAssistant` stripping: when `llm_output` overrides `assistantTexts`, text/content/errorMessage are cleared from `lastAssistant` to prevent reasoning blocks and error metadata from leaking downstream, while preserving `stopReason` and `usage` for failover logic
  - Plugin-blocked errors are excluded from overflow and rate-limit heuristics in both `run.ts` and `agent-runner-execution.ts`
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Hook event shapes are unchanged (additive result types only). Plugins that return `void` behave identically to before. No changes to other hooks, plugin discovery, or the Plugin SDK public surface.

## Change Type (select all)

- [ ] Bug fix
- [x] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #
- Related #57585 — adds a complementary `transform_context` hook at the context-window layer; this PR operates at the prompt-assembly/response-delivery layer instead
- [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause / Regression History (if applicable)

N/A

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can now return `{ block: true, blockReason: "..." }` to prevent an LLM call from being made.
- Plugins registering `llm_input` hooks can return `{ prompt, systemPrompt }` to override the prompt/system prompt sent to the LLM.
- Plugins registering `llm_output` hooks can return `{ assistantTexts: [...] }` to replace the assistant response (e.g. for redaction).
- Existing plugins that return `void` from these hooks are unaffected — fully backward compatible.
- When `allowPromptInjection=false` is set for a plugin, `llm_input` prompt/systemPrompt fields are stripped from the hook result (block/blockReason still honored).

## Diagram (if applicable)

```text
Before:
[prompt ready] -> llm_input (fire-and-forget, no return) -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> llm_output (fire-and-forget) -> [delivered]

After:
[prompt ready] -> await llm_input -> [block? -> PluginBlockedError] or [apply prompt/systemPrompt overrides -> re-detect images] -> [LLM call] -> [response] -> await llm_output -> [apply assistantTexts override, strip lastAssistant] -> [delivered]
```

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: Plugins can now modify prompts and block/alter LLM responses. This is gated behind the existing plugin hook registration mechanism — only installed and enabled plugins can register these hooks. The blocking path throws a dedicated `PluginBlockedError` sentinel that is logged and excluded from failover/overflow misclassification. Prompt modifications are subject to `allowPromptInjection` policy enforcement. Hook payloads are cloned to prevent mutation. Response overrides strip original content from `lastAssistant` to prevent data leaks. Prompt/response modifications flow through the same trust boundary as existing modifying hooks (e.g. `before_tool_call`).

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: macOS
- Runtime/container: Node 22+ / Bun
- Model/provider: Any
- Integration/channel (if any): N/A
- Relevant config (redacted): Plugin with `llm_input`/`llm_output` hook registered

### Steps

1. Register a plugin with an `llm_input` hook that returns `{ block: true, blockReason: "test" }`
2. Trigger an agent run
3. Observe the LLM call is blocked with the logged reason

### Expected

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

### Actual

- LLM call is blocked and error is thrown with reason

## Evidence

- [x] Code review hardening: all 12 review comments from automated reviewers (Codex/Greptile) addressed across 9 follow-up commits
- [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [x] Trace/log snippets: manual gateway test with a local test plugin confirmed `llm_input` and `llm_output` hooks fire correctly on v2026.4.1 gateway. Logs show `llm_input fired` with prompt rewrite (`-> rewriting prompt (prepending tag)`), `llm_output fired` with `assistantTexts (1)` and usage data, and `-> BLOCKING: prompt contains "BLOCKED"` for the block path. The released gateway treats hooks as fire-and-forget (return values ignored), confirming backward compatibility; modifying behavior (block/rewrite/redact) is validated by 22 automated tests.
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

## Human Verification (required)

- Verified scenarios: Hook blocking, prompt override, response redaction via test plugins, multi-plugin priority chaining with `evolveEvent` propagation
- Manual gateway testing: installed a local test plugin (`llm-hook-test`) on a live v2026.4.1 gateway via `openclaw plugins install -l`. Confirmed hook registration, `llm_input` firing with prompt data (provider, model, sessionId, imagesCount), `llm_output` firing with assistantTexts and usage. Tested block, rewrite, redact, and observe scenarios through the dashboard web UI. Verified the released gateway's fire-and-forget path is unaffected (backward compat).
- Automated tests: 22/22 tests pass in `wired-hooks-llm-modifying.test.ts` covering block result, prompt/systemPrompt override, evolveEvent propagation, multi-plugin priority merge, backward compat (void returns), and error handling (graceful degradation).
- Edge cases checked: Plugin returning void (backward compat), hook failure graceful degradation (logged warning, continues), `allowPromptInjection=false` constraint, image re-detection failure rollback, empty prompt override (`prompt: ""`), `PluginBlockedError` bypassing failover/overflow heuristics
- What you did **not** verify: End-to-end production load with many concurrent plugins across multiple providers

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: A misbehaving plugin could block all LLM calls or silently rewrite prompts.
  - Mitigation: Blocking is logged with reason via dedicated `PluginBlockedError`. Prompt/response modifications are opt-in (plugin must explicitly return override fields). `allowPromptInjection=false` can constrain prompt modification. Hook payloads are cloned. Existing hook failure handling catches and logs errors gracefully without crashing the run. Higher-priority plugins take precedence via first-setter-wins merge strategy.

target.title: Onboarding improvement: prompt Codex users to enable native web search
target.body: ## Summary When onboarding/configure detects OpenAI Codex OAuth or another Codex-capable default, offer native Codex web search first. This lets Codex users enable the built-in native search path without choosing a separate managed provider (but still allows to do so, which I am happy to remove to simplify?) If they decline it, continue into the existing managed-provider flow so providers like Perplexity still remain available. it is possible that we should instead just add this as an option to the list of search providers - open to feedback ## Change Type (select all) - [ ] Bug fix - [x] Feature - [ ] Refactor required for the fix - [ ] Docs - [ ] Security hardening - [ ] Chore/infra ## Scope (select all touched areas) - [ ] Gateway / orchestration - [ ] Skills / tool execution - [ ] Auth / tokens - [ ] Memory / storage - [ ] Integrations - [ ] API / contracts - [x] UI / DX - [ ] CI/CD / infra ## Linked Issue/PR - Closes # - Related # - [ ] This PR fixes a bug or regression ## User-visible / Behavior Changes See screenshots linked above, TL;DR suggests to enable built in web search instead of taking user through the search providers selection ## Security Impact (required) - New permissions/capabilities? (`No`) - Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`No`) - New/changed network calls? (`No`) - Command/tool execution surface changed? (`No`) - Data access scope changed? (`No`) This has been possible before with configuration, just exposing during onboarding ## Repro + Verification ### Environment - OS: macOS - Runtime/container: local dev checkout via `pnpm`, disposable config dir created by `scripts/tmp- onboard-codex-web-search.fish` - Model/provider: OpenAI Codex OAuth, default Codex-capable model (`openai-codex/gpt-5.4`) - Integration/channel (if any): none - Relevant config (redacted): ```json { "auth": { "profiles": { "": { "provider": "openai-codex" } } }, "tools": { "web": { "search": { "enabled": true } } } } ### Steps 1. Run onboarding in a fresh disposable env with scripts/tmp-onboard-codex-web-search.fish. 2. During onboarding, choose OpenAI Codex OAuth. 3. Continue to web search setup. 4. Accept or decline the native Codex web search prompt. ### Expected - If onboarding already established OpenAI Codex auth/model context, prompt the user to enable native Codex web search. - If the user declines, continue into the existing managed-provider flow so providers like Perplexity can still be selected. ### Actual - Before this change, onboarding only offered managed search providers and did not surface the native Codex web search option. - After this change, onboarding shows the native Codex web search prompt for Codex-relevant setups, and declining it still leads to the managed-provider picker. ## Evidence Attach at least one: - [ ] Failing test/log before + passing after - [ ] Trace/log snippets - [x] Screenshot/recording (see above) - [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant) ## Human Verification (required) ### Verified scenarios - Fresh onboarding in a disposable env now shows the native Codex web search prompt after choosing OpenAI Codex OAuth. - Search is working with no API keys provided - Declining the native Codex prompt still reaches the managed-provider selection flow. - `pnpm test src/commands/configure.wizard.test.ts` passed. - Targeted onboarding coverage for enabling native Codex web search passed. - pnpm build passed. ### Edge cases checked: - Prompt is gated to Codex-relevant setups. - Configure and onboarding now share the same prompt/helper path. - Managed providers remain available if the user backs out of native Codex search. setting up with non-codex-oauth flow does goes straight into web search provider flow ## Review Conversations - [ ] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR. - [ ] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment. If a bot review conversation is addressed by this PR, resolve that conversation yourself. Do not leave bot review conversation cleanup for maintainers. ## Compatibility / Migration - Backward compatible? (`Yes`) - Config/env changes? (`No`) (sort of since onboarding changes config but does not add new config?) - Migration needed? (`No`) ## Risks and Mitigations No risks, 100% bug free code, I asked codex to make no mistakes 🫠

target.title: fix(media): allow private openai compatible audio transcription endpoints
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Audio media-understanding transcription could target self-hosted OpenAI-compatible endpoints, but audio config did not expose the private-network request opt-in used by guarded provider HTTP.
- Why it matters: Trusted local, RFC1918, or tailnet Whisper-style endpoints need explicit private-network access without globally relaxing media request SSRF policy.
- What changed: Added audio-only request config support for `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, aligned schema/generated metadata/docs, threaded audio request overrides into provider execution, and aligned OpenAI-compatible loopback audio auth with the chat-completion local auth pattern by using the shared `custom-local` marker only for loopback OpenAI-compatible audio bases.
- What did NOT change (scope boundary): Image and video media request config still reject `allowPrivateNetwork`, private-network access remains disabled by default, explicit `request.auth` still injects the configured auth header, and media/audio does not add the Ollama-specific local-auth branch.

## Change Type (select all)

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [x] Docs
- [ ] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope (select all touched areas)

- [ ] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [x] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [x] Integrations
- [x] API / contracts
- [x] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes N/A
- Related N/A
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause (if applicable)

- Root cause: Audio media config reused the generic media request schema/type path, which rejected `allowPrivateNetwork`, while provider execution needed an audio-specific trusted request override path. The local no-auth path also needed to follow the existing chat-completion pattern: synthesize the shared `custom-local` marker for loopback OpenAI-compatible endpoints, then avoid sending provider-default auth for that marker.
- Missing detection / guardrail: Existing schema and runner tests did not cover audio-specific private-network request overrides or loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints without provider auth.
- Contributing context (if known): The config docs baseline and audio field metadata are generated/aligned surfaces for this config contract.

## Regression Test Plan (if applicable)

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test or file: `src/config/schema.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts`, `src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- Scenario the test should lock in: Audio config accepts and forwards `allowPrivateNetwork`, image/video reject it, per-model audio request config works, loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can synthesize `custom-local` when provider auth is absent, synthetic local audio auth sends no provider-default `Authorization`, explicit request auth still wins, and Ollama does not get the media/audio local-auth branch.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: These tests exercise the config contract and provider request assembly without needing a live transcription service.
- Existing test that already covers this (if any): N/A
- If no new test is added, why not: N/A

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Users can opt audio transcription requests into private-network endpoints with `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork` and per-model audio request config. Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio endpoints such as `http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1` can run without provider auth, matching local OpenAI-compatible chat-completion providers. The OpenAI provider docs now show the loopback example base URL.

## Diagram (if applicable)

N/A

## Security Impact (required)

- New permissions/capabilities? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- New/changed network calls? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Command/tool execution surface changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- Data access scope changed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If any `Yes`, explain risk + mitigation: The new network capability is an explicit audio-only opt-in for trusted private endpoints; defaults remain blocked by the SSRF guard and image/video still reject the private-network flag. Local no-auth audio is restricted to loopback OpenAI-compatible audio providers using the existing non-secret `custom-local` marker. Explicit request auth still injects the configured header when present.

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- OS: Linux 6.1.0-44-cloud-amd64 x86_64
- Runtime/container: Node v22.22.2, pnpm 10.33.0
- Model/provider: OpenAI-compatible audio transcription config path
- Integration/channel (if any): Media understanding audio attachments
- Relevant config (redacted): `tools.media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork`, `tools.media.audio.request.auth`, `tools.media.audio.models[].baseUrl`

### Steps

1. Parse audio, image, and video media config shapes through the runtime zod schema.
2. Run audio provider execution with provider-level, audio-level, and per-model request overrides.
3. Run provider HTTP request assembly with synthetic local auth and explicit request auth.

### Expected

- Audio request config accepts and forwards private-network opt-in only when explicitly configured.
- Image/video reject `allowPrivateNetwork` under media request config.
- Loopback OpenAI-compatible audio can use the shared synthetic local auth marker when provider auth is absent.
- Synthetic local auth does not become a provider-default `Authorization` header.
- Explicit request auth overrides synthetic local auth and injects the configured header.
- Ollama does not receive a media/audio local-auth special case.

### Actual

- Matches expected in local tests and changed-gate validation.

## Evidence

- [x] Failing test/log before + passing after
- [ ] Trace/log snippets
- [ ] Screenshot/recording
- [ ] Perf numbers (if relevant)

Local validation:

- `pnpm docs:list`
- `pnpm test src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/shared.test.ts`
- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/model-auth.ts src/media-understanding/runner.entries.ts src/media-understanding/runner.proxy.test.ts src/media-understanding/runner.test-mocks.ts docs/providers/openai.md docs/nodes/audio.md CHANGELOG.md src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.ts src/media-understanding/openai-compatible-audio.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `pnpm check:changed`

## Human Verification (required)

What you personally verified (not just CI), and how:

Set up a (WhisperLive)[https://github.com/collabora/WhisperLive] server locally. Modified openclaw.json to point to it with `media.audio.request.allowPrivateNetwork = true`:

```
"media": {
      "audio": {
        "enabled": true,
        "language": "en",
        "models": [
          {
            "provider": "openai",
            "model": "whisper-1",
            "baseUrl": "http://127.0.0.1:8000/v1",
          }
        ],
        "request": {
          "allowPrivateNetwork": true,
        }
      }
```

Verified audio transcription:

```
$ openclaw capability audio transcribe --file tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav --language en --json
{
  "ok": true,
  "capability": "audio.transcribe",
  "transport": "local",
  "attempts": [],
  "outputs": [
    {
      "path": "/home/openclaw/.openclaw/workspace/tmp/media-test/transcription-test.wav",
      "text": "Open Claw Media Transcription Test. The passphrase is Brass Lobster 7.",
      "kind": "audio.transcription"
    }
  ]
}
```



## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

No PR review conversations currently exist.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? (`Yes/No`) Yes
- Config/env changes? (`Yes/No`) Yes, optional new audio request config only
- Migration needed? (`Yes/No`) No
- If yes, exact upgrade steps: N/A

## Risks and Mitigations

- Risk: Users could point audio transcription at private endpoints they do not fully trust.
  - Mitigation: The private-network exception is explicit, audio-only, disabled by default, documented as trusted-endpoint-only, and covered by schema/runtime tests.
- Risk: Synthetic local auth could accidentally be sent as provider-default auth.
  - Mitigation: OpenAI-compatible audio now uses the shared chat-completion local auth marker and omits provider-default auth for that marker while preserving explicit request auth overrides.

target.title: docs(skills/peekaboo): clarify Bridge architecture for subprocess agents
target.body:
## Summary

- Adds a `Subprocess agents (Bridge architecture)` section to `skills/peekaboo/SKILL.md`.
- Explains why an agent reading `peekaboo permissions status --no-remote` may see "Not Granted" even when the user has correctly granted Screen Recording + Accessibility to the host app — the two checks evaluate different processes.
- Tells subprocess agents (gateway-spawned Node, agent runtimes, cron jobs) to always use the default Bridge path and never `--no-remote`.
- Clarifies that the macOS Sequoia "bypass private window picker" dialog is a separate escalation owned by `loginwindow`, not by the host app.
- Tabulates anti-patterns I have actually seen subprocess agents do (raw `screencapture`, `--no-remote`, telling the user to grant SR to the user's terminal/Node binary).

Docs-only change. No code, tests, or config touched.

## Why

A subprocess agent running under an OpenClaw deployment hit `PERMISSION_ERROR_SCREEN_RECORDING`. The user had already granted SR + AX to `OpenClaw.app` (Bridge host shows green). The agent walked through:

1. `screencapture -x ...` → `could not create image from display`
2. `peekaboo list screens` → works (no capture, just enumeration)
3. `peekaboo image --mode screen` → `PERMISSION_ERROR_SCREEN_RECORDING`
4. User grants SR + AX to OpenClaw.app
5. `peekaboo permissions status` → `Source: Peekaboo Bridge`, both granted
6. Capture via Bridge succeeds
7. Agent re-tests with `peekaboo permissions status --no-remote` → still "Not Granted"
8. Reports the state as "split between Bridge and CLI"

Step 7 is technically correct (`--no-remote` checks the calling Node process's TCC, which is empty by design) but misframed. The agent and the user both wasted cycles trying to grant SR to the wrong process. The current SKILL.md says "Requires Screen Recording + Accessibility permissions" without explaining that the grants belong to the Bridge host, not to the subprocess. The new section closes that gap.

## Real behavior proof

**Setup:** macOS 15.x (Sequoia), `peekaboo 3.0.0-beta4`, OpenClaw running with `ai.openclaw.mac.debug` granted SR + AX via System Settings.

**Reproducing the diagnostic split this section addresses:**

```
$ peekaboo permissions status
Source: Peekaboo Bridge
Screen Recording (Required): Granted
Accessibility (Required): Granted

$ peekaboo permissions status --no-remote
Source: local runtime
Screen Recording (Required): Granted   # depends on caller — varies per process
Accessibility (Required): Not Granted

$ peekaboo bridge status --verbose | grep "Selected:"
Selected: remote gui via /Users/.../clawdbot/bridge.sock (build 2026.5.6)

$ peekaboo image --mode screen --screen-index 0 --json | grep success
"success" : true                       # Bridge path works
```

The two `permissions status` outputs reflect different processes. Both are valid simultaneously. A subprocess agent reading the second output as a permission problem is misreading the architecture, and the SKILL.md previously did not steer them away from that interpretation.

**Verified each anti-pattern in the new table** by running the discouraged command and the recommended alternative on this setup. Each pair behaves as the table claims (Bridge path succeeds; `--no-remote` from a process without its own SR grant fails as expected).

**Skill rendering:** Reviewed the rendered Markdown locally; the ASCII Bridge-selection diagram, the rules block, the diagnostic flow, and the anti-pattern table all render correctly in standard GitHub Markdown.

## Not tested

- macOS 14 / Sonoma. The "bypass private window picker" prompt is Sequoia-specific; the rest of the section applies regardless.
- Configurations where `Peekaboo.app` or `Claude.app` is the resolved Bridge host (priority order 1 or 2). Behavior is documented in the existing `bridge` command help and applies the same way; only the host name changes.

## AI-assisted disclosure

Drafted with Claude (Opus 4.7) from a real diagnostic session. Reviewed and verified by hand against a live OpenClaw + Peekaboo Bridge setup on the machine where the original agent confusion occurred.
